unit.name: Mechanical Maintenance Service Line
unit.cfj: Restore mechanical integrity of production equipment through scheduled and reactive repair

meta.method: Ulwick ODI (Importance × Satisfaction → Opportunity), needs scoring v2

stakeholders.title: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of returning production equipment to service with unresolved mechanical integrity defects
rows.plain: Make it less likely that production equipment is put back into use while physical problems that could affect its condition are still unresolved. This helps prevent equipment failure or loss of product protection.
rows.imp_rat: Returning equipment with unresolved integrity defects is the direct antithesis of the CFJ; severity is high due to downstream failure and quality loss.
rows.sat_rat: Release checklists and QA sign-off catch many defects but latent or intermittent faults routinely escape into service. Verification steps and sign-offs add handback time yet defects still slip through when checks are rushed. Detecting unresolved defects before release relies on inspector experience that varies and is not always available. Returning equipment with hidden defects triggers rework and lost production, a costly outcome the status quo only partly guards against.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of production equipment releases made with unresolved mechanical integrity defects
rows.plain: Avoid releasing production equipment when it has known mechanical problems that have not been fixed. This helps prevent breakdowns and interruptions to production.
rows.imp_rat: Duplicate of the core release-with-defects error; directly central to CFJ with high severity from field failures.
rows.sat_rat: Standard release-control practice reduces defective releases but a measurable percentage still pass through. Release reviews add lead time but defective releases still recur despite them. Reliable defect-free release depends on skilled QA and maintenance judgement that is inconsistently applied. Each defective release drives rework and downtime costs that current controls only partially contain.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that restored equipment fails mechanically during scheduled fill-finish production
rows.plain: Avoid having repaired equipment break down during planned production. This helps keep the production schedule on track.
rows.imp_rat: Mechanical failure of restored equipment during fill-finish is the exact failure the job exists to prevent (high relevance); severity is high given lost sterile batches and line stoppage.
rows.sat_rat: Standard PM programs and functional checks reduce but do not consistently prevent mechanical failures during scheduled production, so surprise failures still occur. Preventive maintenance schedules and post-repair run-in periods add time but do not eliminate in-service failures during a scheduled campaign. Reliable restoration depends heavily on experienced technicians whose judgement about wear limits and root cause is uneven across shifts. An unexpected mechanical failure mid-campaign carries very high cost from lost batches, but that is exactly what current practice fails to reliably prevent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of critical equipment degradation remaining undetected until batch quality or line continuity is affected
rows.plain: Avoid letting serious equipment wear or damage go unnoticed until it harms product quality or stops the production line. Finding problems early helps reduce disruption and loss.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected critical degradation affecting batch quality or line continuity is central; severity high combining product loss and downtime.
rows.sat_rat: Predictive/condition-based monitoring is uneven, so critical degradation often surfaces only when line continuity or quality is already hit. Condition monitoring can give early warning but manual inspection intervals leave long undetected windows. Interpreting degradation signals demands specialized reliability engineering skill that many sites lack in depth. Undetected degradation reaching batch impact is extremely costly, and current detection only partly averts it.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that restored equipment with latent mechanical degradation is accepted
rows.plain: Avoid approving repaired equipment when hidden mechanical damage or wear is still present. This helps ensure the repair really restored the equipment to dependable working order.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting equipment with latent degradation defeats the verification purpose of restoration; severity is high because failure surfaces later during production.
rows.sat_rat: Standard acceptance testing and inspection catch gross faults but routinely miss subtle latent degradation, leaving a recurring gap in confidence. Latent-defect detection is not primarily a timing problem, and rushing acceptance actually worsens it, so time is not the dominant lens here. Detecting latent degradation depends heavily on experienced inspectors and diagnostic judgement that is unevenly distributed across service crews. Accepting equipment with hidden degradation triggers expensive downstream failures, but the immediate acceptance decision is not chiefly a cost exercise.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a sterility-critical unresolved condition is not escalated before equipment release
rows.plain: Make sure any unresolved problem that could affect product sterility is reported to the right people before the equipment is released.
rows.imp_rat: Failing to escalate a sterility-critical unresolved condition before release is central to the release gate; severity catastrophic — sterile product released on compromised equipment.
rows.sat_rat: Sterility-critical conditions are sometimes released without escalation, a high-consequence gap. Escalating sterility-critical conditions adds hold time before release. Recognizing sterility-critical issues requiring escalation needs aseptic judgement. An unescalated sterility issue risks major batch and compliance costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of viable microbial contamination introduced onto exposed product-contact surfaces during maintenance
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of living germ contamination placed on surfaces that touch the product during maintenance.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing viable microbial contamination onto product-contact surfaces is central to sterile-area maintenance; severity catastrophic — direct patient risk.
rows.sat_rat: Microbial introduction is invisible and hard to prevent consistently, making this poorly assured. Sanitization and controlled handling add time to every product-contact intervention. Aseptic technique to avoid microbial introduction demands trained, disciplined behavior. Contamination events cause expensive batch rejection.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination entering sterile product-contact areas during repair work
rows.plain: Prevent germs from entering areas where the product touches equipment while repairs are being done. This protects the product from being contaminated during maintenance.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering sterile product-contact areas during repair is central to fill-finish restoration; severity catastrophic — direct patient safety and batch loss.
rows.sat_rat: Standard contamination-control practices are mature and largely effective, though ingress during active repair remains an inherent residual hazard. Containment setup and post-work cleaning add time that is planned into sterile repairs. Preventing product-contact contamination requires skilled aseptic technique and vigilance during the intervention. Cleaning, sanitization and monitoring impose recurring cost that is accepted for sterile work.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that contaminants from maintenance migrate into adjacent active sterile filling areas
rows.plain: Keep dirt, germs, and other unwanted material from maintenance work from reaching nearby areas where sterile product is being filled. This protects exposed product during repairs or upkeep.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminants migrating into adjacent active sterile filling areas is highly relevant in fill-finish; severity catastrophic — cross-contamination of active production.
rows.sat_rat: Cross-area contamination control is imperfect and consequences are severe. Containment measures add setup time near active filling areas. Preventing contaminant migration requires understanding of airflow and aseptic zoning. Migration into active filling can force costly line shutdowns.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of mechanical repair work compromising the sterile boundary in the fill-finish area
rows.plain: Make repairs without damaging the sealed, germ-free area where medicine is filled. This helps prevent contamination after the repair is finished.
rows.imp_rat: Compromising the sterile boundary during repair is highly relevant in fill-finish restoration; severity is catastrophic — patient-safety and full batch/line contamination.
rows.sat_rat: Rigorous aseptic work permits and interventions practices substantially protect the boundary, though human error during repairs remains a real residual risk. Sterile-boundary protective procedures lengthen repair jobs considerably but that delay is an accepted control. Preserving the sterile boundary during mechanical work demands highly specialized aseptic-repair competence that is scarce. Containment, gowning and re-qualification costs are high but treated as non-negotiable in fill-finish.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a sterile equipment boundary is reassembled with gaps or misaligned seals after repair
rows.plain: After a repair, make sure the clean equipment is put back together with tight, correctly lined-up seals and no openings. This prevents germs from getting inside.
rows.imp_rat: Reassembling a sterile boundary with gaps or misaligned seals is central to fill-finish repair; severity catastrophic — sterility compromise of product.
rows.sat_rat: Sterile boundary integrity after repair is hard to confirm and failures carry high consequence. Verifying sterile-boundary seals adds inspection time before handback. Ensuring gap-free aseptic seals requires specialized aseptic reassembly knowledge. A compromised boundary can force costly batch loss and re-sterilization.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that aseptic or product-contact boundary damage remains undetected during qualification
rows.plain: Make sure damage to the clean product area or to surfaces that touch the medicine is found during testing and checks. Undetected damage could allow contamination later.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected aseptic/product-contact boundary damage during qualification is highly relevant in fill-finish; severity catastrophic due to sterility breach.
rows.sat_rat: Integrity and leak testing catch gross damage well, yet subtle boundary defects still escape detection at times. Boundary integrity tests add test time but are normally scheduled within qualification. Detecting aseptic or product-contact boundary damage requires specialized inspection skill that is limited and uneven. Specialized boundary testing has real cost but the governing concern is undetected contamination risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of repairing the wrong mechanical cause during a reactive intervention
rows.plain: Prevent workers from fixing the wrong mechanical problem during an urgent repair. This helps restore the equipment correctly and avoid making the problem worse.
rows.imp_rat: Repairing the wrong cause in reactive work fails to restore integrity (high relevance); severity high because the true fault recurs and downtime repeats.
rows.sat_rat: Misdiagnosis remains common in reactive work, making this a chronic weak point. Chasing the wrong cause wastes significant diagnostic and repair time. Correct root-cause identification hinges heavily on scarce diagnostic expertise under production pressure. Wrong repairs waste parts and labor and prolong downtime.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that latent mechanical-integrity defects remain undetected under the recommended approach
rows.plain: The recommended testing approach should be able to find hidden mechanical weaknesses. This helps prevent equipment from failing even though it appeared to pass the checks.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected latent defects under the recommended approach undermine verification; severity high because they surface as production failures.
rows.sat_rat: Latent defects by definition evade routine qualification, so standard practice leaves a real recurring gap here. Detecting latent defects requires added test cycles that lengthen qualification but are planned for. Anticipating latent mechanical-integrity defects demands deep failure-mode expertise that is scarce and inconsistently applied. Extra inspection and testing consume resources, but the dominant risk is undetected failure rather than spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterile product batches lost after restored equipment loses mechanical integrity
rows.plain: Reduce the number of production runs of germ-free product that must be thrown away when repaired equipment later fails. This saves product and reduces the cost of using the equipment over time.
rows.imp_rat: Sterile batches lost after restored equipment loses integrity is a direct, costly outcome; severity high — significant product and financial loss.
rows.sat_rat: When restored equipment loses integrity mid-run, sterile batches are lost, a severe and recurring reliability failure. Batch loss is a consequence of failure, not a matter of task duration. Preventing batch loss depends on reliable restoration more than on any single scarce skill. Lost sterile batches are extremely expensive, making this need cost-heavy, but the trigger is integrity loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of production equipment released with unresolved integrity-critical defects
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of production equipment put into use while still having serious unresolved defects that could affect its condition. This helps prevent equipment failures and production problems.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing equipment with unresolved integrity-critical defects directly negates the CFJ of restoring mechanical integrity; consequences include in-production failure and contaminated batches.
rows.sat_rat: Formal work-order sign-off and QA release gates in GMP environments catch most integrity-critical defects but latent or misjudged defects still slip through occasionally. Release-hold and defect-review steps add time but are routinely built into the workflow so timing is not the primary limiter here. Deciding what counts as integrity-critical still leans on experienced maintainers and quality judgement that is unevenly available across shifts. Holding equipment for defect closure carries production-loss cost, which pressures teams to release borderline cases.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of maintenance provisions permitting sterile-boundary compromise in fill-finish areas
rows.plain: Prevent maintenance work in filling areas from creating a way for germs to enter the protected product area. This helps preserve the product’s cleanliness during repairs and servicing.
rows.imp_rat: Maintenance provisions that permit sterile-boundary compromise are central to fill-finish repair design; severity catastrophic due to contamination of sterile product.
rows.sat_rat: Design reviews and risk assessments catch many provision-level risks but latent design gaps in maintenance access still occasionally permit compromise. Reviewing maintenance provisions for boundary risk adds design and planning time that is usually budgeted. Judging whether a maintenance provision could permit a boundary breach requires specialized contamination-control engineering expertise. Designing out boundary-compromising provisions is capital-intensive but justified by patient-risk avoidance.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of latent mechanical degradation remaining unidentified until production failure
rows.plain: Find gradual wear or damage in equipment before it causes a production breakdown. This helps prevent unexpected downtime.
rows.imp_rat: Latent degradation persisting until production failure defeats the inspection purpose; severity high due to unexpected in-service failure.
rows.sat_rat: Inspection and condition-monitoring programs catch much degradation but latent faults still frequently remain hidden until failure. Condition monitoring and inspection intervals govern how quickly latent degradation surfaces, and gaps between checks allow delay. Interpreting subtle degradation signals demands scarce diagnostic and reliability-engineering expertise. Predictive monitoring technology and analysis carry cost that limits how comprehensively it is deployed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of mechanical degradation progressing undetected to in-service equipment failure
rows.plain: Detect equipment wear or damage before it grows into a failure during use. This helps prevent breakdowns and unsafe operation.
rows.imp_rat: Degradation progressing undetected to in-service failure is the core detection failure of the job; severity high with production impact.
rows.sat_rat: Some degradation is caught by PdM but progression to in-service failure still occurs frequently on unmonitored or poorly-instrumented equipment. Trend detection can lag actual degradation because monitoring is periodic rather than continuous on many assets. Recognizing a progressing failure mode from monitoring data requires experienced condition-analysis judgement. Full continuous monitoring coverage is cost-prohibitive so many assets rely on cheaper periodic checks.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of filled units rejected for equipment-induced container or closure defects after restoration
rows.plain: Reduce the number of filled containers rejected because equipment damages the container or its seal after the equipment is repaired or put back into service.
rows.imp_rat: Filled units rejected for equipment-induced container/closure defects show restoration failed to protect product; severity high due to batch/unit loss.
rows.sat_rat: Post-restoration setup verification catches many issues but equipment-induced defects still reach filled product. Container/closure defect detection may lag, adding rework time after the fact. Correct setup to avoid closure defects requires precise skilled adjustment that varies. Rejected filled units are direct scrap cost that restoration quality strongly influences.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that production equipment returns to service before restored mechanical integrity is verified
rows.plain: Make it less likely that equipment is used again before it has been confirmed to be properly repaired and safe. This matters to prevent the same failure from happening again.
rows.imp_rat: Returning equipment before integrity is verified undercuts the verification core of the job; severity high as unverified equipment can fail in production.
rows.sat_rat: Documented release procedures exist but are inconsistently enforced, so equipment sometimes returns before verification is complete. Formal verification gates before restart consume time but are frequently compressed under production pressure. Judging whether integrity is truly verified needs experienced reviewers who are not always present at handback. Skipping verification to save time risks costly reintroduced failures, an ever-present trade-off today.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that equipment meeting qualification criteria loses mechanical integrity during validated use
rows.plain: Make it less likely that equipment passes its required tests but becomes damaged or unsafe while being used as approved. This matters so the equipment remains reliable during production.
rows.imp_rat: Equipment that passes qualification but loses integrity during validated use points to inadequate restoration; severity high due to production failure.
rows.sat_rat: Point-in-time qualification poorly predicts later in-service integrity loss, leaving a persistent exposure. In-use integrity loss surfaces over operating time that no single qualification event can span. Predicting degradation under validated use requires reliability-engineering judgement rarely embedded in maintenance qualification. Preventing in-use loss through added monitoring adds cost, but the felt loss is the failure itself.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that restored equipment fails mechanical integrity or functional checks
rows.plain: After repair, the equipment should still be sound and work as intended. This helps prevent another failure from extending the time the work is stopped.
rows.imp_rat: Restored equipment failing integrity or functional checks means the job output is defective; severity high forcing rework or masking failure.
rows.sat_rat: Functional and integrity checks are standard but restored equipment still fails them at a notable rate. Post-repair functional checks add time yet do not guarantee passing integrity checks. Passing integrity checks first time depends on skilled repair execution that varies by technician. Failed checks force repeat work and delayed cost, which happens often enough to matter.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that approved corrective action leaves the mechanical failure mechanism unresolved
rows.plain: An approved fix should address the underlying cause of the equipment failure, not just its visible effects. This helps keep the same failure from happening again.
rows.imp_rat: Corrective action leaving the failure mechanism unresolved means the job's purpose is not met; severity high due to certain recurrence.
rows.sat_rat: Corrective actions are often symptomatic, leaving the true failure mechanism intact and recurrence likely. An unresolved failure mechanism resurfaces, consuming repeat repair time. Ensuring corrective action truly addresses the mechanism demands strong analytical skill that is scarce. Ineffective corrective actions repeat cost cycles.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of maintenance-related foreign objects remaining inside production equipment after repair
rows.plain: Reduce the number of tools, bits of material, or spare parts left inside equipment after repairs. This helps prevent foreign objects from getting into the product.
rows.imp_rat: Foreign objects left inside equipment after repair is a direct maintenance defect; severity high — equipment damage and product contamination.
rows.sat_rat: FOD still escapes into equipment despite checklists, a persistent risk. Post-repair inspection for foreign objects adds a closeout step. Thorough FOD checks depend on discipline more than specialized skill. A retained object can wreck equipment and product at high cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that hidden internal deterioration escapes detection during mechanical inspection
rows.plain: Make sure hidden damage inside the equipment is found during inspection. This helps prevent a machine that looks acceptable on the outside from failing or causing problems.
rows.imp_rat: Hidden internal deterioration escaping inspection defeats detection; severity high — undetected defect surfaces as production failure.
rows.sat_rat: Hidden internal deterioration frequently escapes routine inspection. Detecting hidden deterioration can require slow, thorough inspection. Finding concealed internal wear demands high inspection expertise and sometimes NDT. Undetected deterioration leads to costly failures later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that verification evidence misrepresents the restored mechanical condition
rows.plain: Make sure the proof of inspection accurately shows the equipment's condition after it was repaired or put back together. This helps prevent an incomplete restoration from being accepted.
rows.imp_rat: Verification evidence misrepresenting restored condition corrupts the release decision; severity high — defective equipment released on false assurance.
rows.sat_rat: Verification methods can produce misleading readings, so trust in the evidence is only moderately assured. Verification evidence accuracy is about correctness, not the speed of producing it. Ensuring evidence truly represents restored condition needs skilled test design and interpretation. Misrepresenting condition causes costly false acceptance, but the core failing is evidence integrity.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of maintenance activity introducing contamination into the sterile production environment
rows.plain: Prevent maintenance work from bringing dirt or germs into the sterile production area. This helps protect products while equipment is being serviced.
rows.imp_rat: Maintenance introducing contamination into the sterile environment is central to fill-finish repair; severity catastrophic — sterility and patient risk.
rows.sat_rat: Strong cleanroom protocols and controls make maintenance-induced contamination rare where practices are mature. Contamination controls add gowning and cleaning time to every intervention. Aseptic maintenance technique demands specialized training that not all technicians hold. A contamination event is extremely costly, driving heavy investment in controls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of returning serviced equipment to production with a mechanical condition outside required tolerances
rows.plain: Prevent serviced equipment from being returned to production when its mechanical condition is outside the required limits. This helps the equipment work safely and correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Returning equipment outside required tolerances is a direct integrity defect; severity high as it leads to malfunction and quality impact.
rows.sat_rat: Some out-of-tolerance conditions still slip through to production, a recurring gap. Final tolerance verification before release consumes bench and test time. Judging whether conditions meet tolerance needs measurement skill and interpretation. Releasing out-of-tolerance equipment causes costly scrap and re-intervention.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of maintenance-generated particulate matter released into the sterile production environment
rows.plain: Limit the dust and other tiny particles released into the sterile work area during maintenance. This helps keep the production environment clean.
rows.imp_rat: Maintenance particulate released into the sterile environment is a direct contamination concern; severity high due to sterility and batch risk.
rows.sat_rat: Maintenance inherently generates particulate that is hard to fully contain in a cleanroom. Containment and cleanup of particulate extends the intervention. Minimizing particulate generation requires disciplined aseptic technique. Particulate excursions can invalidate the sterile environment at high cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of maintenance-affected critical parameters omitted from qualification
rows.plain: Include all important settings affected by maintenance when checking that a process still works properly. This helps ensure maintenance has not made the process unreliable.
rows.imp_rat: Maintenance-affected critical parameters omitted from qualification leaves changes unverified; severity high — undetected validated-state impact.
rows.sat_rat: Parameter omission is a persistent qualification gap only partly addressed by checklists. Identifying all affected critical parameters takes analysis time within scoping. Tracing maintenance effects to critical parameters requires equipment and process knowledge that is scarce. The effort cost is minor versus the risk of an omitted parameter.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of incorrect reassembly compromising alignment or sealing integrity
rows.plain: Put equipment back together correctly so its parts line up and seal properly. This helps prevent leaks, poor operation, or contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect reassembly compromising alignment/sealing is a direct restoration defect (high relevance); severity high given leaks, sterility loss, and rework.
rows.sat_rat: Reassembly errors are a frequent source of post-repair failures despite procedures. Careful reassembly and checking alignment adds time versus rushing. Correct alignment and sealing depends on technician craftsmanship and torque discipline. Rework from bad reassembly wastes seals, gaskets and labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of contamination-control breaches by maintenance personnel during sterile-area repairs
rows.plain: Maintenance workers should avoid bringing germs or other contamination into a very clean work area while making repairs. This helps protect the products being made there.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination-control breaches by mainteners in sterile areas are highly relevant to fill-finish repair; severity high — sterility loss and patient risk.
rows.sat_rat: Established aseptic behavior training and monitoring reduce breaches, yet personnel-driven lapses recur enough to remain a live concern. Contamination-control gowning and behavior protocols slow sterile-area repairs but are consistently enforced. Avoiding contamination breaches depends heavily on trained, disciplined personnel behavior which is the dominant vulnerability. Training, gowning and environmental monitoring costs are substantial but routine.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of scheduled fill-finish units not produced because restored equipment cannot sustain planned throughput
rows.plain: Avoid missing planned production because repaired equipment cannot keep working fast enough for the schedule. This helps keep the expected number of units on time.
rows.imp_rat: Restored equipment unable to sustain planned throughput means integrity restoration fell short; severity high due to unmet fill-finish schedule and lost units.
rows.sat_rat: Restored equipment frequently returns able to run but not at full committed throughput, an unreliably managed gap. Throughput shortfalls from underperforming restored equipment extend recovery time but are not the primary loss. Achieving full planned throughput after repair relies on skilled calibration and setup that is inconsistent. Lost units represent significant unrealized value that current practice cannot fully protect.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical mechanical-integrity attributes omitted from qualification criteria
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment approval requirements include all important features that show the equipment is mechanically sound. This helps prevent buying equipment that may fail or affect production.
rows.imp_rat: Critical integrity attributes omitted from qualification criteria means verification is blind to real risks; severity high as latent defects pass.
rows.sat_rat: Omitted critical attributes are a recurring authorship weakness that templates only partially mitigate. Building complete criteria lists takes upfront effort but is done during protocol drafting. Knowing which critical attributes must appear in criteria depends on validation and equipment expertise that varies by author. The cost is in engineering time to define criteria, not material spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of maintenance interventions with an unrecorded impact on sterile product-contact boundaries or aseptic conditions
rows.plain: Reduce the number of maintenance jobs whose effect on sterile product-contact areas or clean working conditions was not recorded. This helps show whether the product and work area remained protected.
rows.imp_rat: Unrecorded impact on sterile boundaries/aseptic conditions hides contamination risk; severity high — untraceable sterility compromise.
rows.sat_rat: Impacts on sterile boundaries are frequently under-documented. Recording sterile-boundary impacts adds documentation time. Knowing which interventions affect aseptic boundaries requires understanding. Unrecorded impacts create compliance and investigation costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of mechanical failures attributed to symptoms rather than underlying causes
rows.plain: Reduce the number of mechanical breakdowns blamed on visible symptoms instead of their true underlying causes. This helps prevent the same failures from happening again.
rows.imp_rat: Attributing failures to symptoms rather than causes leaves the real fault untreated; severity high due to recurrence.
rows.sat_rat: Without disciplined root-cause methods, many failures are treated at the symptom level and recur. Chasing symptoms rather than causes wastes time, but the root error is diagnostic depth, not clock time. Distinguishing symptom from root cause is fundamentally a high-skill diagnostic act that depends on scarce experienced analysts. Symptom-only fixes generate repeat spend, yet the immediate failing is analytical rather than budgetary.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that exposed sterile product-contact surfaces remain open to the surrounding environment during repair
rows.plain: Keep exposed surfaces that touch the clean product open to the surrounding air for as little time as possible during repairs. This lowers the chance that those surfaces become contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Prolonged open exposure of sterile product-contact surfaces raises contamination probability; severity high due to sterility risk.
rows.sat_rat: Exposure windows are managed but variable, leaving residual risk. Minimizing open-surface exposure time is the core objective and is hard to compress. Efficient work to limit exposure duration depends on technician planning. Prolonged exposure raises contamination-driven batch-loss risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of consequential degradation and failure mechanisms omitted from the maintenance strategy
rows.plain: Leave out fewer important ways that equipment could wear out or fail from the maintenance plan. This helps maintenance address problems before they cause serious trouble.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting consequential failure mechanisms from the maintenance strategy causes systematic under-maintenance; severity high — unaddressed failure modes.
rows.sat_rat: RCM/FMEA practices capture major mechanisms but less obvious ones are commonly missed from strategies. FMEA-style analysis to enumerate failure mechanisms is time-consuming but done periodically. Identifying all consequential degradation mechanisms depends heavily on deep equipment-specific expertise. Thorough failure-mode analysis costs engineering time that is often constrained.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of lubricant or maintenance chemical residue left on product-contact surfaces after repair
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of oil, lubricant, or repair chemical left on surfaces that touch the product. This helps keep those substances out of the product.
rows.imp_rat: Lubricant/chemical residue on product-contact surfaces is a specific contamination hazard; severity high — chemical contamination of sterile product.
rows.sat_rat: Residual lubricant or chemical on surfaces is frequently missed, a recurring contamination gap. Wiping and cleaning residue adds cleanup time on product-contact surfaces. Knowing acceptable residue limits and cleaning methods needs some training. Residue can contaminate product causing batch loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of tools and replacement parts entering the sterile area with unacceptable contamination
rows.plain: Reduce the number of tools and replacement parts that enter the sterile area carrying too much dirt or germs. This helps protect the area and product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Tools/parts entering the sterile area with unacceptable contamination is a direct contamination source; severity high — sterility breach.
rows.sat_rat: Tool and part contamination on entry still occurs despite entry procedures. Cleaning and gowning tools/parts before entry adds prep time. Proper decontamination of items entering sterile areas needs aseptic training. Contaminated items entering can trigger costly excursions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the underlying mechanical failure mechanism remains unidentified after investigation
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that an investigation ends without finding what caused the machine to fail. Knowing the real cause helps prevent the same failure from happening again.
rows.imp_rat: Failure mechanism unidentified after investigation means restoration cannot durably succeed; severity high due to recurrence.
rows.sat_rat: Formal RCA exists but is inconsistently rigorous, so failure mechanisms frequently remain unidentified. Incomplete investigations shortcut analysis time but defer the real delay to recurrence. Identifying the underlying mechanism is a deeply skill-dependent task where expertise is the binding constraint. Missed root cause guarantees repeat cost that better analysis would avoid.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of recurring mechanical integrity failures within the required service interval
rows.plain: Reduce repeated failures that affect the strength and proper working condition of equipment during the time it is supposed to remain in service. This shows whether the repair or replacement actually worked.
rows.imp_rat: Recurring failures within the required service interval indicate restoration fell short of the committed reliability; severity high — repeated outages.
rows.sat_rat: Failures recurring inside the required interval indicate the restoration did not reliably hold. Recurring failures within the interval shorten effective service life, but the error is the recurrence itself. Avoiding early recurrence hinges on correct root-cause repair, which is skill-limited. Premature recurrence forces unplanned spend, though reliability is the dominant concern.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of contributing mechanical faults overlooked when a failure has interacting causes
rows.plain: Find as many of the mechanical problems contributing to a failure as possible, especially when several problems interact. This helps prevent an incomplete repair.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking contributing faults in interacting-cause failures leaves partial restoration; severity high through recurrence and misdiagnosis.
rows.sat_rat: Multi-cause failures routinely leave contributors unaddressed. Overlooked contributing faults trigger repeat interventions later. Spotting interacting causes demands advanced diagnostic reasoning that is scarce. Missed contributors cause repeat failures and added spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of repeat mechanical integrity failures after production equipment returns to service
rows.plain: Reduce the chances that the equipment will fail again after it has been repaired and put back into use. This avoids repeated repairs, delays, and added cost.
rows.imp_rat: Repeat integrity failures after return to service show restoration didn't hold; severity high through recurring downtime and quality risk.
rows.sat_rat: Repeat integrity failures after return to service remain a common and painful outcome today. Repeat failures cost uptime, but the defining error is recurrence, not duration. Preventing repeats depends on getting the diagnosis and repair right, a skill-dependent outcome. Each repeat failure incurs fresh repair and downtime cost, yet reliability of the fix is the core issue.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of completed repairs failing defined mechanical-integrity requirements
rows.plain: Reduce the share of completed repairs that fail the required checks for strength and proper operation. This helps ensure repaired equipment remains dependable.
rows.imp_rat: Completed repairs failing integrity requirements means the job's output is defective; severity high as it forces rework or masks a latent risk.
rows.sat_rat: Standard acceptance testing and sign-off catch most non-conforming repairs, yet a meaningful fraction still pass through undetected before service. Post-repair verification adds duration but is normally scheduled into the job so time is secondary. Correctly verifying that a repair meets integrity specs depends on skilled inspection and correct acceptance criteria being applied. Rework of failing repairs consumes labour and parts but is generally accepted as a manageable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that return to service is approved despite critical qualification failure
rows.plain: Make sure equipment is not approved for use again when it has failed an important required check. This helps prevent unsafe or unreliable equipment from returning to service.
rows.imp_rat: Approving return despite qualification failure is central to compliant handback; severity is high given regulatory and product-safety consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Multi-layer approval and QA oversight make outright approval-despite-failure uncommon, but human review gaps mean it is not eliminated. Formal release-approval steps are scheduled events that rarely add unplanned delay. Correctly recognizing a critical qualification failure before approval leans on senior QA and validation expertise that is not uniformly available. The cost of the sign-off process is trivial next to the consequences of approving a failed qualification.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which completed mechanical restoration deviates from approved requirements
rows.plain: Make completed repairs match the approved requirements as closely as possible. This helps ensure the repair restores the equipment in the way it was meant to.
rows.imp_rat: Completed restoration deviating from approved requirements is a direct conformance defect; severity high — non-compliant, potentially unsafe equipment.
rows.sat_rat: Work instructions and QA reduce deviations but restored work still departs from approved requirements at times. Deviations from approved requirements trigger rework delays. Conforming precisely to specs needs disciplined skilled execution. Non-conforming work drives repeat cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable quality and mechanical-integrity requirements omitted from the maintenance strategy
rows.plain: Reduce the number of important quality and equipment-safety requirements left out of the maintenance plan. This helps ensure the equipment is kept safe and reliable.
rows.imp_rat: Quality/integrity requirements omitted from the maintenance strategy is a systemic planning gap; severity high — repeated undetected defects.
rows.sat_rat: Structured maintenance-strategy development captures most requirements yet omissions still slip through review. Compiling applicable requirements into strategy takes upfront effort but is a one-time-ish task. Knowing which quality and integrity requirements apply demands specialized regulatory and engineering knowledge. Requirement-mapping effort has a cost but is modest relative to consequences of omission.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of mechanical fault diagnoses in which a symptom is mistaken for the root cause
rows.plain: Reduce the share of equipment investigations where a visible sign of a problem is mistaken for the underlying cause. This helps ensure the real problem is fixed.
rows.imp_rat: Mistaking symptom for root cause means the real fault is untreated; severity high — recurrence and wasted intervention.
rows.sat_rat: Symptom-vs-cause confusion is common and poorly guarded against. Mistaking symptom for cause wastes time on ineffective repairs. Distinguishing symptom from root cause is fundamentally an expertise-dependent judgement. Treating symptoms wastes parts and recurs at cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that restored equipment repeats the same mechanical fault before the committed operating interval ends
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that repaired equipment develops the same mechanical problem again before the planned operating period ends. This helps keep production running.
rows.imp_rat: Repeating the same fault before the committed operating interval indicates failed restoration; severity high due to unplanned downtime and recurring cost.
rows.sat_rat: Without robust root-cause closure the same fault recurs before the committed interval ends fairly often. Repeat faults within the operating interval force unplanned re-entry that disrupts the schedule. Preventing recurrence needs strong root-cause skill that is often shortcut under time pressure. Repeat failures double repair cost and lost output, a recurring drain today.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that GMP return is recommended with critical qualification deviations unresolved
rows.plain: Do not recommend returning to normal production while important qualification problems remain unresolved. This supports a decision that can be clearly justified to regulators.
rows.imp_rat: Recommending GMP return with unresolved qualification deviations is central to compliant restoration; severity is high due to regulatory exposure and unsafe release.
rows.sat_rat: Established change-control and QA sign-off practice catches most unresolved critical deviations, though occasional escapes still occur under schedule pressure. Deviation-review gates before GMP-return recommendation add review cycles but are routinely built into the disposition timeline. Catching unresolved critical deviations depends heavily on experienced QA reviewers whose judgement varies across sites. The review overhead is modest compared to the cost of a wrongful release, so cost is not the binding pain.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of mechanically critical components omitted from an inspection
rows.plain: Make sure every mechanically important part is included in the inspection. This helps prevent hidden equipment problems from being missed.
rows.imp_rat: Critical components omitted from inspection means integrity is unverified where it matters; severity high — undetected defect passes to production.
rows.sat_rat: Critical components are sometimes omitted from inspection despite checklists. Complete inspection coverage takes time that gets compressed. Knowing all critical components to inspect depends on knowledge and checklists. Omitted inspections let failures through at later cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of equipment failures recurring after repair closure
rows.plain: Prevent equipment from failing again after it has been repaired. This reduces repeated interruptions and the need for more repairs.
rows.imp_rat: Recurring failures after repair closure indicate the restoration didn't hold; severity high through repeated downtime and rework.
rows.sat_rat: Repair closure processes exist but recurrence after closure is a persistent and commonly experienced problem. Root-cause analysis after repair takes time that is often shortchanged under production pressure. Preventing recurrence depends on skilled root-cause analysis that many teams apply inconsistently. Recurring failures waste repeated parts and labour, a cost driver that is often tolerated.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical fill-finish equipment units simultaneously unavailable because of mechanical faults
rows.plain: Keep multiple important filling machines from being out of service at the same time because of mechanical problems. This helps production continue without major delays.
rows.imp_rat: Multiple critical units down simultaneously from mechanical faults reflects restoration/availability failure; severity high due to major production loss.
rows.sat_rat: Some redundancy and spares exist but coincident mechanical faults on critical units still strand the line. Concurrent outages compound downtime duration well beyond planned windows. Managing simultaneous failures stretches scarce maintenance expertise thin across units. Multiple critical units down at once causes major production loss that redundancy rarely fully offsets.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of repaired equipment experiencing the same mechanical failure before the next scheduled intervention
rows.plain: Prevent equipment that has just been repaired from having the same mechanical problem again before its next planned service. This helps avoid unexpected stoppages.
rows.imp_rat: Same failure recurring before next intervention shows incomplete restoration; severity high through repeated outages within the committed interval.
rows.sat_rat: Standard repair practice reduces but does not reliably prevent same-mode recurrence before scheduled intervention. Whether a fault recurs before the next intervention depends on interval timing that is often not tuned to failure evidence. Correctly diagnosing and fully resolving the underlying mechanism requires expertise not always present at repair time. Repeat failures within an interval waste the earlier repair investment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of production equipment failing before the planned maintenance intervention
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that production equipment breaks down before its planned maintenance. This helps avoid unexpected stoppages and disrupted production.
rows.imp_rat: Equipment failing before planned maintenance points to inadequate preventive restoration; severity high through unplanned downtime and product impact.
rows.sat_rat: Preventive schedules reduce pre-interval failures but unexpected failures before planned maintenance remain common. Planned interval timing versus actual failure timing is a chronic mismatch that lead time cannot fully close. Setting intervals that pre-empt failure needs reliability-engineering skill that is unevenly applied. Premature failures force costly unplanned interventions and production loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of recurrence of the same mechanical failure after production restart
rows.plain: Reduce how often the same mechanical problem happens again after production starts back up. This helps keep equipment running and avoids another production stoppage.
rows.imp_rat: Recurrence of the same failure after restart shows restoration didn't address the cause; severity high due to repeated downtime and product impact.
rows.sat_rat: Same-fault recurrence after restart is a well-known persistent problem. Recurrence forces a second full intervention, doubling downtime. Preventing recurrence depends on correct root-cause resolution requiring expertise. Repeat failures multiply parts and labor spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of recurrence of the same mechanical failure after repaired equipment returns to production
rows.plain: Reduce how often the same mechanical problem happens again after repaired equipment is put back into production. This helps prevent repeated repairs and interruptions.
rows.imp_rat: Recurrence after return to production reflects incomplete restoration; severity high through repeated outages and quality risk.
rows.sat_rat: Repeat failures after return to production remain a chronic reliability gap. Recurring failures repeatedly consume repair and outage time. Durable fixes require diagnostic depth that is unevenly available. Recurrence drives repeated component and labor costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of mechanical safeguards left absent or mispositioned when repaired equipment is handed back
rows.plain: Make sure all safety guards are put back in the right place when repaired equipment is returned. This helps protect workers from moving or dangerous parts.
rows.imp_rat: Safeguards left absent or mispositioned at handback is both integrity and safety relevant; severity high — injury risk when equipment operates.
rows.sat_rat: Missing or mispositioned safeguards at handback still occur despite checklists. Reinstalling all guards adds a closeout step. Guard reinstallation is straightforward but attention-dependent. Guards are low cost; a missing guard risks injury and stoppage.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of affected validated functions omitted from requalification scope
rows.plain: Reduce the number of important equipment or process functions left out of the checks required to confirm they still work properly. This helps ensure the full process remains reliable.
rows.imp_rat: Affected validated functions omitted from requalification leaves changes unverified; severity high — unvalidated functions in GMP use.
rows.sat_rat: Functions are still occasionally omitted from requalification scope despite standard impact assessment. Enumerating affected validated functions extends scoping but is routine work. Determining which validated functions need requalification depends on validation expertise that varies. Cost is analysis labor, not the driving concern.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the recommended scope omits GMP-critical restoration effects
rows.plain: Lower the chance that the recommended checking plan leaves out important effects of restoring the equipment or process. This helps ensure all important manufacturing-related changes are properly checked.
rows.imp_rat: Recommended scope omitting GMP-critical restoration effects is a planning defect; severity high due to compliance gaps and unaddressed integrity effects.
rows.sat_rat: Impact-assessment practice catches many effects but scope omissions remain a common finding. Scoping restoration effects adds assessment time but is part of the standard impact review. Recognizing GMP-critical restoration effects requires cross-disciplinary expertise that is not always present. Scoping effort is inexpensive relative to consequences of a missed critical effect.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that restored equipment returns to sterile fill-finish production before qualification acceptance
rows.plain: Make sure repaired equipment is tested and approved before it is used again to make germ-free products. This helps prevent unready equipment from affecting product safety.
rows.imp_rat: Returning to sterile production before qualification acceptance is central to compliant restoration; severity high given sterility and regulatory risk.
rows.sat_rat: Equipment status labeling, lockout, and release-gate controls are mature for sterile lines and make premature return rare. Interlocks and status-tagging prevent premature restart without materially extending the timeline. Enforcing that sterile fill-finish equipment stays down until acceptance relies on disciplined change-control administration rather than rare expertise. Holding equipment out of service costs production time but that trade-off is accepted for sterile lines.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in contamination-control status when repaired equipment is returned to production
rows.plain: Make it clear whether repaired equipment is still protected from contamination when it returns to production. This helps people know whether it is safe to use.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in contamination-control status at return to production risks releasing unclear sterile condition; severity high — sterility uncertainty.
rows.sat_rat: Contamination-control status at return is often ambiguous. Clarifying contamination status at return adds handoff time. Communicating clear contamination-control status requires knowledge and diligence. Ambiguous status forces precautionary re-cleaning costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict between acceptance thresholds and the approved validated state
rows.plain: Keep the rules for accepting something from conflicting with the approved condition it was tested to meet. This helps ensure the item is judged against the right requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Conflict between acceptance thresholds and the validated state can pass non-compliant equipment; severity high — regulatory and quality exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Threshold-to-validated-state conflicts are a known recurring authorship error only partly caught in review. Reconciling thresholds against the validated state adds review iterations to protocol approval. Aligning acceptance thresholds with the approved validated state requires validation expertise that is unevenly applied. The reconciliation cost is minor compared with the risk of conflicting limits.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of stored mechanical energy releasing from springs, counterweights, or raised assemblies during maintenance
rows.plain: Prevent springs, weights, or raised parts from suddenly moving during maintenance. This reduces the risk of someone being struck, trapped, or injured.
rows.imp_rat: Stored-energy release is a safety hazard adjacent to the repair task rather than the integrity outcome, but severity is catastrophic — severe or fatal injury from springs/counterweights.
rows.sat_rat: Stored-energy sources are less consistently covered than electrical LOTO, leaving real residual risk. Bleeding down or blocking stored energy adds modest time when the hazard is recognized. Identifying and neutralizing springs, counterweights and raised loads demands experienced judgement that is not uniformly present. Blocking devices and restraints are inexpensive relative to injury cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the recommended approach conflicts with applicable GMP or site validation requirements
rows.plain: Make sure the recommended method does not conflict with required manufacturing rules or the site’s own proof that the process works as intended. This helps avoid approval problems later.
rows.imp_rat: Recommended approach conflicting with GMP/site validation is a compliance failure; severity high — regulatory violation and unsafe release.
rows.sat_rat: Mature regulatory review and SOP frameworks make outright conflicts with requirements fairly uncommon. Checking GMP and site-validation alignment adds compliance review time already built into approval. Interpreting applicable GMP and site validation requirements demands regulatory expertise concentrated in QA/validation staff. Compliance review is low-cost relative to a regulatory nonconformance.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required mechanical tolerance points left unverified before release
rows.plain: Check all required points on the equipment before releasing it for use. This helps confirm that the equipment is built and working within the allowed limits.
rows.imp_rat: Unverified tolerance points before release is a gap in the verification step; severity high as unchecked points can hide integrity defects.
rows.sat_rat: Verification coverage is incomplete, leaving points unchecked at release. Verifying every tolerance point takes time that is sometimes shortcut. Knowing which points require verification and measuring them needs competence. Unverified points that fail later cost re-intervention.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical fill-finish repair tasks lacking demonstrated workforce proficiency
rows.plain: Make sure people doing important repairs on products that must stay germ-free have shown they can do the work correctly. This reduces mistakes during repairs.
rows.imp_rat: Critical fill-finish tasks lacking demonstrated proficiency is a competence gap; severity high — incompetent work in sterile-critical areas.
rows.sat_rat: Qualification programs cover core tasks but demonstrated proficiency for all critical fill-finish tasks is inconsistently evidenced. Building proficiency takes training time but the deficiency itself is a competence gap. Critical fill-finish repairs demand demonstrated specialized proficiency that is hard to build and retain. Qualifying and re-qualifying specialists is costly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between protocol criteria and validated equipment limits
rows.plain: Make sure the test instructions fit within the equipment's approved operating limits. This prevents asking the equipment to do something it was not designed or approved to do.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between protocol criteria and validated limits can accept non-compliant equipment; severity high — regulatory and quality exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Mismatches between criteria and validated limits still slip through despite standard cross-checks. Cross-checking protocol criteria to equipment limits lengthens review but is routine. Matching criteria to validated limits leans on documented specifications and reviewer diligence. Cost is limited to review labor, not a dominant factor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unrecognized pinch, crush, or shear points created by partial disassembly
rows.plain: Reduce the number of hidden places where a person could be pinched, crushed, or cut when equipment is partly taken apart. This helps protect people doing maintenance.
rows.imp_rat: Unrecognized pinch/crush/shear points from partial disassembly is a safety hazard adjacent to the integrity task; severity high — serious personnel injury.
rows.sat_rat: Newly created mechanical hazards are inconsistently identified during dynamic work. Identifying new pinch points takes brief hazard assessment. Recognizing hazards created by partial disassembly needs safety awareness. Unrecognized pinch points risk injury with associated costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of repair actions introducing mechanical-integrity defects through incorrect skill application
rows.plain: Incorrect use of skills should not lead to frequent repair work that damages the equipment. Properly trained workers can reduce these avoidable defects.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing defects through incorrect skill application directly corrupts the restoration output; severity high — new integrity defects created.
rows.sat_rat: Qualification and supervision reduce skill-driven defects but variable proficiency still introduces them. Skill-induced defects are not primarily a timing issue. These defects arise directly from incorrect skill application, making competence the dominant lever. Skill-caused rework wastes parts and labour but that is secondary to the competence gap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of susceptible production equipment left exposed to a known mechanical failure mechanism
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of production equipment still exposed to a known type of mechanical failure. This helps prevent the same equipment problem from happening again.
rows.imp_rat: Susceptible fleet left exposed to a known failure mechanism reflects incomplete systemic restoration; severity high — multiple potential failures.
rows.sat_rat: Known failure mechanisms are often addressed reactively unit-by-unit, leaving similar equipment exposed. Fleet-wide remediation of a known mechanism takes long, leaving units exposed meanwhile. Identifying all susceptible units requires engineering knowledge that is inconsistently applied. Deferred fleet fixes save short-term cost but risk larger failure cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between reported restoration status and actual readiness for scheduled production
rows.plain: Reduce the difference between saying that equipment or a work area has been restored and its actual readiness for planned production. This helps prevent production from being scheduled before everything is truly ready.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between reported and actual readiness undermines the handback decision; severity high — production started on equipment that isn't truly ready.
rows.sat_rat: Reported restoration status routinely diverges from actual readiness because reporting is manual and optimistic. Mismatched status reports cause last-minute schedule scrambles that waste time. Accurate status judgement requires experienced interpretation of ambiguous readiness signals. Acting on wrong status wastes staged production resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of equipment acceptance criteria conflicting with approved quality or regulatory requirements
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment’s approval requirements do not conflict with approved safety, quality, or legal requirements. This prevents equipment from being accepted on the wrong basis.
rows.imp_rat: Acceptance criteria conflicting with quality/regulatory requirements can pass non-compliant equipment; severity high — compliance exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory alignment checks exist but conflicts between local criteria and approved requirements still surface periodically. Reconciling criteria against quality and regulatory requirements takes review time but is scheduled. Cross-checking against regulatory requirements needs regulatory literacy that specialists provide. Mostly a review-labor cost with little material impact.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of deviation of local airflow and pressure conditions from accepted sterile-area limits during maintenance
rows.plain: Keep air movement and air pressure in the local area within the accepted limits during maintenance. This helps protect the clean area from unwanted contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Airflow/pressure deviation from sterile limits during maintenance is a contamination-control concern; severity high — sterility of the area at risk.
rows.sat_rat: Local airflow and pressure are readily disturbed during maintenance and hard to hold. Managing airflow and pressure during work adds setup time. Maintaining sterile airflow limits during maintenance requires specialized understanding. Airflow deviations can invalidate the environment at high cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of production equipment starting or moving while personnel occupy a mechanical danger zone during maintenance
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that production equipment starts or moves while someone is in a dangerous mechanical area for maintenance. This helps prevent serious injury.
rows.imp_rat: Equipment moving into a danger zone while personnel are present is peripheral to the mechanical restoration act itself but severity is catastrophic — crushing or fatal injury to maintainers.
rows.sat_rat: Mature LOTO practice makes accidental energization rare where it is enforced, though human lapses still occur. LOTO procedures add setup time but are a standard, budgeted step in every job. Lockout/tagout is a well-trained, procedure-driven task that most technicians execute reliably. Locks, tags and isolation hardware are cheap and standard-issue.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that equipment remains in service above the accepted production-risk threshold
rows.plain: Reduce how long equipment continues operating when the risk to production is higher than allowed. This helps limit the chance and impact of a production problem.
rows.imp_rat: Equipment running above accepted risk threshold is a risk-exposure duration concern; severity high because failure during that window can be severe.
rows.sat_rat: Risk thresholds exist but enforcement lags, leaving equipment in service too long. Equipment lingering above risk threshold prolongs the exposure window before action. Judging when threshold is breached needs risk-assessment expertise that is uneven. Running over threshold risks costly failure that early action would avoid.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of production disruption propagated from a single interconnected equipment failure
rows.plain: Limit how much one equipment failure interrupts the rest of production. This helps keep a single problem from causing a wider shutdown.
rows.imp_rat: Disruption propagating from one interconnected failure is a resilience issue adjacent to single-unit restoration; severity high due to cascading line loss.
rows.sat_rat: Some isolation and redundancy exist but single-point failures still propagate disruption fairly often. Cascading failures extend recovery time well beyond a single asset repair. Understanding interconnection risk requires systems-level engineering insight that is scarce. Redundancy and isolation to limit propagation are capital-heavy investments often deferred.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of interacting mechanical contributors left unidentified in the failure diagnosis
rows.plain: When something fails, fewer possible mechanical causes should remain unexplained. This makes it easier to find and fix the real problem.
rows.imp_rat: Interacting contributors left unidentified in diagnosis yields partial fixes; severity high due to recurrence.
rows.sat_rat: Single-cause thinking leaves interacting contributors unidentified, undermining lasting restoration. Finding interacting contributors takes longer, but the shortfall is analytical capability rather than duration. Identifying multiple interacting mechanical contributors requires sophisticated systems-level diagnostic skill that few possess. Missed contributors cause rework cost, yet the primary gap is expertise, not money.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a mechanical condition exceeding the assigned work scope goes unrecognized
rows.plain: Make sure a machine problem that is beyond your assigned work is noticed and reported. This helps the right person handle it instead of letting it go unaddressed.
rows.imp_rat: An out-of-scope condition going unrecognized means a real defect is missed; severity high — untreated integrity issue and escalation failure.
rows.sat_rat: Scope-boundary recognition is inconsistent and often depends on individual judgement. Failing to recognize out-of-scope conditions delays proper escalation. Judging when a condition exceeds assigned scope requires broad competence. Unrecognized scope creep risks improper repairs and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of tools, fasteners, or debris items left unaccounted for after repair
rows.plain: Leave fewer tools, screws, and pieces of waste behind after a repair. This helps prevent lost items, contamination, and problems during later work.
rows.imp_rat: Unaccounted tools/fasteners/debris after repair is a specific FOD control need; severity high due to equipment damage and contamination risk.
rows.sat_rat: Tool-control practices help but unaccounted items still occur when discipline lapses. Tool and part accounting adds a short reconciliation step at closeout. Tool tallying is a low-skill but attention-dependent practice. Shadow boards and tally kits are inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a removed or loosened component falling onto personnel during hands-on repair
rows.plain: Prevent a part that is being removed or loosened during repair from falling onto a person. This reduces the chance of injury during hands-on maintenance.
rows.imp_rat: A falling component during repair is a task-safety concern peripheral to integrity outcome, but severity is high — serious crush injury to personnel.
rows.sat_rat: Dropped-component incidents still occur because supporting practices are inconsistently applied. Rigging and supporting components before removal adds time that is often improvised. Judging which components will drop and rigging them safely relies on technician experience. Slings, jacks and supports are low cost and generally on hand.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of mechanical failure investigations concluded before causal evidence is sufficient
rows.plain: Avoid ending investigations into equipment failures before there is enough reliable information to explain what caused them. Waiting for sufficient evidence leads to better decisions about preventing the failure again.
rows.imp_rat: Concluding investigations before sufficient evidence risks wrong diagnosis; severity high through recurrence from premature closure.
rows.sat_rat: Investigations concluded on insufficient evidence yield unreliable conclusions and repeat failures. Investigations are often cut short under schedule pressure, so time pressure contributes but is not the defining error. Knowing when causal evidence is sufficient is a judgement call demanding investigative expertise that is unevenly available. Premature closure saves short-term cost but creates larger downstream expense, so cost is secondary.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of maintainers being authorized for repair tasks despite unproven competence
rows.plain: Make sure only people who have proven they can do the repair work are allowed to perform it. This helps prevent unsafe or incorrect repairs.
rows.imp_rat: Authorizing maintainers with unproven competence is an upstream control failure; severity high because incompetent work produces integrity defects and safety risk.
rows.sat_rat: Qualification-matrix and authorization controls in GMP sites are fairly mature so unproven-competence authorizations are relatively well contained. Authorization gating adds minor administrative time but is not the core issue. The need is about ensuring proven competence before authorizing repair tasks, squarely a skill-assurance concern. Maintaining and verifying qualifications carries administrative cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that restored equipment fails production acceptance at scheduled handback
rows.plain: Make sure repaired equipment passes the required checks when it is returned for production. Otherwise, production may not be able to restart as planned.
rows.imp_rat: Failing production acceptance at handback is central to the release gate; severity moderate since it is caught before use but delays production.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance testing is standard but restored equipment still fails at handback often enough to be a known risk. Failing production acceptance at handback forces additional delay before restart. First-pass acceptance depends on skilled repair and pre-checks that are inconsistently thorough. Acceptance failures generate rework cost and idle-line cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that mechanical restoration causes secondary equipment damage or misalignment
rows.plain: Make sure repairing equipment does not damage other parts or leave them out of position. This helps the equipment work correctly after the repair.
rows.imp_rat: Restoration causing secondary damage or misalignment corrupts the very integrity it aims to restore; severity high — new failure modes introduced.
rows.sat_rat: Repair-induced secondary damage is an occasional but real quality problem tied to technician variability. Secondary damage during repair forces additional unplanned downtime. Avoiding misalignment and collateral damage is highly skill-dependent handwork. Secondary damage adds parts and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required mechanical integrity criteria lacking objective acceptance thresholds
rows.plain: Set clear, measurable pass-or-fail limits for every required check that shows whether equipment is still intact and working properly. This makes it possible to judge restoration work consistently.
rows.imp_rat: Integrity criteria lacking objective thresholds weakens the verification gate; severity moderate-high — subjective accept/reject decisions.
rows.sat_rat: Many integrity criteria still rely on subjective judgement rather than objective thresholds, producing inconsistent decisions. Defining acceptance thresholds is a one-time effort, not a recurring time drain. Setting objective thresholds demands engineering expertise to translate integrity into measurable limits. Missing thresholds cause disputes and rework cost, but the essence is measurement reliability.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of mechanical connections reassembled outside specified alignment or tightness limits
rows.plain: Make sure machine parts are put back together with the correct positioning and tightness. This helps the equipment work safely and properly after maintenance.
rows.imp_rat: Connections outside alignment/tightness limits is a specific reassembly defect central to integrity; severity high — leaks, loosening, and eventual failure.
rows.sat_rat: Torque specs and tools reduce but do not eliminate out-of-limit connections. Torque-checking connections adds bounded time per joint. Correct tightening and alignment is a trainable skill supported by torque tools. Torque wrenches and gauges are inexpensive standard tools.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in criteria for confirming restored mechanical integrity
rows.plain: Make the signs of a properly repaired and sound machine clear and easy to check. This helps the worker know exactly when the repair is complete.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in integrity-confirmation criteria affects reliable verification but is an enabling factor; severity moderate as it raises risk of wrong accept/reject decisions.
rows.sat_rat: Without explicit pass/fail thresholds, integrity confirmations vary between individuals and inconsistent closeouts are common in today's practice. Ambiguous restoration criteria force dispatchers to wait on clarifications, extending closeout time before equipment can be released. Confirming restored integrity relies heavily on experienced technicians' judgement because acceptance criteria are rarely spelled out in the work package. Unclear criteria cause rework and repeat interventions that consume extra labor and parts.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of lingering doubt that the identified mechanical cause explains the recurring equipment failure
rows.plain: Reduce any remaining doubt that the suspected mechanical problem is really causing the equipment to fail repeatedly. This helps people choose the right repair with confidence.
rows.imp_rat: Lingering doubt that the identified cause explains recurring failure signals possible misdiagnosis; severity moderate-high — continued recurrence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the responding shift lacking capability required for an urgent equipment failure
rows.plain: Make sure the workers on duty are likely to have the skills and ability needed to handle a sudden equipment failure. This helps the team respond effectively when urgent problems occur.
rows.imp_rat: A shift lacking capability for an urgent failure delays reactive restoration; severity high due to prolonged outage and error risk from unqualified work.
rows.sat_rat: Shift rostering and on-call arrangements partly cover capability gaps but off-hours competence shortfalls persist. Whether the right capability is present on shift determines response speed, but the gap itself is a competence gap. Urgent failures routinely expose shifts lacking the specific specialized repair competence needed. Maintaining full-capability coverage on every shift is costly and often not funded.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical equipment-to-process dependencies omitted from risk assessments
rows.plain: Include every important way the equipment can affect the work when assessing risks. This helps prevent equipment-related problems from being missed.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting equipment-to-process dependencies from risk assessments weakens scoping; severity high — unassessed impacts propagate to production.
rows.sat_rat: Risk assessments frequently omit critical dependencies because they rely on incomplete process knowledge. Omitted dependencies surface as surprise downtime later. Mapping equipment-to-process dependencies needs deep system expertise. Missed dependencies cause costly cascading disruptions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of operating-condition excursions that recreate the mechanical failure mechanism
rows.plain: Have fewer operating conditions that could cause the same mechanical problem to happen again. This helps prevent repeat equipment failures.
rows.imp_rat: Operating excursions recreating the failure mechanism is partly operations-driven, adjacent to maintenance; severity high due to recurrence.
rows.sat_rat: Operating-condition excursions still recur because controls between maintenance and operations are loosely coupled. Excursions that recreate faults trigger new downtime cycles. Controlling operating conditions requires operator discipline and process knowledge. Recurrence from excursions repeats full failure cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of disagreement among conclusions about whether mechanical integrity has been restored
rows.plain: Reduce disagreement about whether the equipment has been repaired well enough to be sound again. Consistent conclusions help prevent production from restarting too soon or being delayed unnecessarily.
