rows.imp_rat: Disagreement over whether integrity is restored undermines the acceptance decision; severity moderate-high — inconsistent release calls.
rows.sat_rat: Absent shared objective criteria, conclusions about restored integrity vary from person to person. Disagreement over restoration conclusions drags out decisions, but the disagreement itself is the error, not the delay. Reconciling conflicting expert conclusions requires senior judgement that is not always at hand. Prolonged disputes carry cost, yet the primary loss is inconsistent conclusions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a loose tool or damaged component being ejected toward personnel during functional testing
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that a loose tool or broken part will fly toward a worker during testing. This helps prevent injuries.
rows.imp_rat: Ejected tools during testing is a specific safety hazard, less central to the restoration job; severity high due to potential serious injury.
rows.sat_rat: Loose-tool or debris ejection during test is a recurring surprise that standard practice only partly controls. Guarding and clearing during functional testing adds a short verification step. Recognizing ejection hazards during test runs depends on attentive, experienced staff. Barriers and exclusion zones cost little.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that repaired equipment fails functional verification before release
rows.plain: Make sure repaired equipment works as intended before it is put back into use. This prevents a repair that did not fully solve the problem from affecting work.
rows.imp_rat: Failing functional verification before release is central to the verification gate; severity moderate since it is caught before handback but drives rework.
rows.sat_rat: Functional checks catch many issues but failures at verification still recur. Functional verification adds test time before release. Running and interpreting functional checks is a standard technician competence. Failed verification means repeat labor and delayed handback.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of mechanically critical inspection locations that cannot be physically accessed
rows.plain: Make sure important mechanical inspection points can actually be reached and examined. If a point cannot be reached, a problem there may go unnoticed.
rows.imp_rat: Critical inspection locations that can't be accessed leave integrity unverifiable there; severity high — hidden defects at unreachable points.
rows.sat_rat: Some critical locations simply cannot be reached, a persistent design-driven gap. Gaining access to hidden locations can add significant disassembly time. Working around access limits requires ingenuity but is a design constraint. Inaccessible points may hide failures that cost later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of repair events requiring repeat intervention because of incorrect fault diagnosis
rows.plain: Reduce repairs that have to be repeated because the original cause of the problem was identified incorrectly. Finding the real cause the first time saves time and prevents recurring failures.
rows.imp_rat: Repeat intervention from incorrect diagnosis is a rework/efficiency loss central to reactive repair quality; severity moderate — repeated downtime.
rows.sat_rat: Diagnostic aids and procedures help but incorrect diagnosis still commonly drives repeat work. Misdiagnosis forces repeat interventions that consume extra time, a symptom of the underlying skill gap. Accurate fault diagnosis is fundamentally a specialized skill that varies widely among maintainers. Repeat interventions waste labour and parts.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of repairs requiring additional corrective work before mechanical integrity is restored
rows.plain: Reduce the number of repairs that need more work before the equipment is sound and safe again. Finishing repairs correctly the first time helps reduce downtime.
rows.imp_rat: Repairs needing additional corrective work before integrity is restored is a rework/quality issue; severity moderate — schedule and cost, caught before release.
rows.sat_rat: A meaningful share of repairs need extra corrective work, showing inconsistent first-pass quality. Rework loops extend total restoration time beyond plan. First-time-right repair depends on technician skill that varies significantly. Additional corrective work adds labor and parts cost each time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that acceptance criteria remain unchanged after a material restoration-scope change
rows.plain: Make sure the rules for accepting the restored material are reviewed and updated when the amount of restoration changes. This prevents people from using outdated rules to decide whether the work is acceptable.
rows.imp_rat: Acceptance criteria unchanged after a material scope change means the wrong criteria govern acceptance; severity high — acceptance on outdated basis.
rows.sat_rat: Keeping acceptance criteria synchronized with material scope changes is poorly controlled and stale criteria frequently persist. Updating criteria after scope changes adds delay when mid-work changes occur. Recognizing that a scope change invalidates existing criteria requires judgement that is easy to overlook. The rework is a modest labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of production equipment unavailability after an integrity failure
rows.plain: Shorten the time that production equipment is out of use after a failure that lets air or material pass through. This helps resume production sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailability duration after an integrity failure is a timeliness concern for reactive repair; severity moderate — production loss without safety impact.
rows.sat_rat: Repair processes restore equipment but recovery time varies widely with parts and capability availability. The need is explicitly about reducing downtime duration after an integrity failure. Fast restoration depends on skilled responders being available and diagnosing quickly. Extended unavailability carries heavy production-loss cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a replacement component becomes unusable after contamination or loss of packaging integrity before installation
rows.plain: Keep replacement parts protected and usable until they are installed. This prevents contamination or damaged packaging from making a needed part unusable.
rows.imp_rat: A replacement component contaminated or with compromised packaging before install is a contamination-source control; severity high in sterile context.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-install contamination or damage of parts occurs but is often caught. Checking packaging integrity before install adds a brief step. Recognizing compromised parts requires basic inspection knowledge. A ruined part before install wastes its cost and delays work.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of critical replacement components being unavailable after equipment failure
rows.plain: Make sure important replacement parts can be obtained after equipment breaks down. This helps prevent long delays while waiting for repairs.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable critical parts after failure delays restoration but is a logistics enabler; severity high through extended downtime and production loss.
rows.sat_rat: Critical-spares programs cover many items but stockouts for the unexpected failure still occur regularly. Emergency sourcing of critical parts after failure introduces long lead-time delays. Identifying the correct critical spare requires asset knowledge but is not the main constraint. Critical spares inventory is expensive, so stocking decisions constantly trade cost against availability.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of production equipment unavailability caused by mechanical restoration
rows.plain: Shorten the time production equipment is unavailable while it is being repaired or restored. This helps production resume sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Duration of unavailability from restoration is a throughput concern relevant to the job's timeliness; severity moderate — production delay, not safety.
rows.sat_rat: Downtime duration varies widely between predictable PM and unpredictable reactive events. Restoration downtime is actively tracked and managed, but reactive repairs still routinely overrun planned outage windows. Shortening downtime depends on having skilled technicians immediately available, which is not guaranteed. Extended downtime carries lost-production cost that scheduling tries to minimize.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that load-dependent deterioration is masked by the equipment state during inspection
rows.plain: Make sure an inspection does not hide damage that appears only when the equipment is carrying a load. This helps reveal how the equipment actually behaves during use.
rows.imp_rat: Load-dependent deterioration masked by equipment state during inspection causes missed defects; severity moderate-high — undetected latent degradation.
rows.sat_rat: Load-dependent deterioration is often masked at rest, a difficult persistent gap. Recreating load conditions to unmask deterioration takes extra time. Recognizing load-masked deterioration requires advanced diagnostic insight. Masked deterioration escapes and fails at later cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that diagnostic examination alters evidence of the original mechanical failure state
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that examining a failed machine changes the clues showing what originally went wrong. This helps people identify the true cause of the failure.
rows.imp_rat: Diagnostic examination altering original failure evidence hampers accurate root cause; severity moderate — misdiagnosis and recurrence risk.
rows.sat_rat: Diagnostic handling frequently alters the original failure state, corrupting the evidence base. Preserving the original failure state is about integrity of evidence, not speed. Non-destructive examination technique demands trained forensic discipline that is scarce. Altered evidence causes misdiagnosis cost, but the core failing is evidence integrity.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that repair activity continues beyond the assigned work scope before escalation
rows.plain: Repair work should not continue beyond the assigned limits without asking for help or approval. This helps prevent unauthorized changes and additional problems.
rows.imp_rat: Continuing beyond assigned scope before escalation risks unauthorized work; severity high — uncontrolled changes and integrity/compliance impact.
rows.sat_rat: Stop-and-escalate rules exist but are not always followed. Continuing past scope before escalation can extend and complicate the job. Discipline to stop and escalate depends on training and adherence. Unauthorized work beyond scope risks costly errors.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in the restoration acceptance criteria used for the recommendation
rows.plain: Make the rules for deciding whether a repair or restoration is acceptable clearer and less open to different interpretations. This helps people make consistent decisions and avoid unnecessary rework.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in restoration acceptance criteria weakens the recommendation's basis; severity moderate-high — inconsistent accept/reject decisions.
rows.sat_rat: Criteria templates help, but subjective or vaguely worded acceptance language remains a common, unresolved source of variability. Clarifying ambiguous acceptance criteria often triggers back-and-forth that delays the recommendation. Writing unambiguous criteria demands experienced authors, and that expertise is unevenly distributed. Ambiguity rework costs review hours rather than materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time equipment waits for required parts, access, or technical information before restoration can proceed
rows.plain: Shorten the time equipment sits idle while waiting for needed parts, access, or technical information before repairs can continue. This helps restore production more quickly.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for parts, access, or information delays restoration start; severity moderate — extended downtime, not safety.
rows.sat_rat: Availability of parts and information is inconsistent, making wait times unpredictable. Waiting for parts, access, or information is a chronic, large contributor to restoration delay. Expediting scarce parts and info depends on planner experience. Idle equipment awaiting inputs carries heavy opportunity cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of equipment risk assessments based on incomplete or inaccurate condition evidence
rows.plain: Base equipment risk reviews on complete and accurate information about the equipment’s condition. Poor information can lead to unsafe or ineffective decisions.
rows.imp_rat: Risk assessments based on incomplete condition evidence produce unreliable decisions; severity moderate-high — misjudged risk and scope.
rows.sat_rat: Many assessments rest on incomplete or stale condition data, undermining their validity. Poor condition evidence delays sound risk decisions. Gathering and interpreting condition evidence demands specialized skill. Bad-evidence assessments misdirect maintenance spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that maintenance activities proceed outside approved change controls
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that maintenance work is done without following the approved process for reviewing and recording changes. Following that process helps prevent mistakes and unnecessary rework.
rows.imp_rat: Working outside approved change controls is a compliance/governance error somewhat adjacent to the mechanical act; severity high given GMP regulatory exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Established GMP change-control procedures catch most unauthorized work, yet informal or emergency repairs still occasionally slip outside the process. Change-control workflows add review cycles but are routine and expected in the recommendation timeline. Trained quality and engineering reviewers are normally available to screen work against change controls, though vigilance still depends on their attention. Formal change-control systems carry administrative overhead but are already budgeted in GMP environments.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required mechanical expertise is unavailable during the outage
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that a needed mechanical specialist is unavailable during the equipment shutdown. Having the right expertise available can help get the equipment running again sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Required expertise unavailable during the outage delays restoration and risks poor work; severity moderate-high — prolonged downtime and error risk.
rows.sat_rat: Expertise coverage across shifts is uneven, so the needed skill is often not on hand when the fault occurs. Waiting for the right expert to become available prolongs outages. Specialized mechanical expertise is scarce and its unavailability during outages is a core constraint. Contracting emergency specialists is expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required lifecycle conditions absent from mechanical restoration evidence
rows.plain: Make sure the evidence showing that equipment was restored covers every condition it must meet throughout its working life. This helps confirm that the repair or restoration was truly effective.
rows.imp_rat: Missing lifecycle conditions from restoration evidence weakens the completeness of the record; severity moderate-high — questionable release basis.
rows.sat_rat: Required lifecycle conditions are frequently absent from restoration evidence, undermining completeness. Completing lifecycle-condition evidence takes effort but the error is missing conditions, not delay. Knowing which lifecycle conditions to capture requires domain expertise that is unevenly applied. Missing conditions can force costly revalidation, but the core failing is incomplete evidence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of beginning maintenance on the wrong equipment section because its identity or boundary is unclear
rows.plain: Avoid starting maintenance on the wrong part of the equipment because its name or limits are unclear. Clear identification helps protect workers and prevent work on the wrong area.
rows.imp_rat: Starting work on the wrong equipment section due to unclear identity/boundary is a critical setup error; severity high — wrong equipment altered, safety and integrity risk.
rows.sat_rat: Tagging and work-order identification make wrong-equipment starts fairly rare. Confirming equipment identity adds a brief check. Identifying the correct unit is low-skill given tags and labels. Working the wrong equipment wastes labor and risks harm.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time required to identify the failed mechanical component and define the repair scope
rows.plain: Find the broken machine part and determine what repair is needed as quickly as possible. This reduces the time production is stopped.
rows.imp_rat: Time to identify the failed component and define scope affects reactive restoration speed; severity moderate — outage duration, not safety.
rows.sat_rat: Diagnosis quality varies, so scope definition is sometimes wrong and revisited. Diagnosing the failed component and scoping the repair is often a slow, iterative bottleneck. Fast accurate diagnosis is heavily dependent on the diagnostician's experience. Prolonged diagnosis extends costly downtime.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between reported equipment availability and actual production availability
rows.plain: Make the reported equipment availability match whether the equipment can actually be used for production. This helps people rely on uptime reports when planning work.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between reported and actual availability is a planning/communication error; severity moderate — schedule disruption, not safety.
rows.sat_rat: Availability data in CMMS often overstates true production readiness, a persistent trust gap. Discovering availability discrepancies late forces reactive rescheduling. Reconciling reported vs actual availability needs experienced cross-checking that is often absent. Planning around phantom availability wastes committed resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the approved repair scope carried into the work package
rows.plain: Make the approved repair instructions clear about exactly what work is included. This helps workers know what to do without guessing.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in the approved repair scope carried into the work package risks wrong or incomplete work; severity moderate-high — scope errors.
rows.sat_rat: Work packages today frequently carry loosely defined scope, so what gets done varies from what was approved. Scope ambiguity triggers back-and-forth with planners that delays release of the executable package. Interpreting a vague approved scope depends on planner and mechanic experience to fill gaps correctly. Misread scope leads to unnecessary work or omitted work that wastes labor and materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of production disruption caused by equipment handback after its scheduled release point
rows.plain: Limit how long production is interrupted when equipment is returned later than planned. A quicker return helps keep work on schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Late handback disrupting production is a schedule/timeliness concern; severity moderate — production disruption without direct safety impact.
rows.sat_rat: Handback timing is inconsistent, with overruns being a regular occurrence rather than an exception. Late handback directly extends production disruption and is a common, poorly bounded delay. Meeting the release point depends on coordinated skilled execution that frequently slips. Every hour past scheduled release compounds lost-production cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of transient mechanical failure evidence lost before diagnostic examination
rows.plain: Keep temporary clues about a machine problem from disappearing before someone can inspect it. These clues help identify what caused the failure.
rows.imp_rat: Transient failure evidence lost before examination weakens diagnosis; severity moderate — harder root cause, possible recurrence.
rows.sat_rat: Because capture is rarely fast enough, transient failure evidence is regularly lost before examination. Transient evidence decays quickly, so the dominant loss is time elapsing before it can be captured. Capturing transient evidence needs skilled rapid response, but timing is the binding constraint. Lost transient evidence causes costly reinvestigation, yet the essence is a race against time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required replacement components are unavailable when repair begins
rows.plain: Have all needed replacement parts available before repair starts, so the equipment is not left waiting for supplies.
rows.imp_rat: Replacement components unavailable at repair start is a logistics delay; severity moderate — outage extension, duplicate of prior part items.
rows.sat_rat: Critical spares are stocked but stockouts of the right part at the right time still happen frequently. Missing components at repair start directly extend downtime. Correct spares forecasting requires inventory and reliability skill. Emergency sourcing of components inflates cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an intermittent mechanical fault is absent during the available diagnostic window
rows.plain: Make sure a fault that happens only sometimes is noticed during the time available for checking it, so the real problem is not missed.
rows.imp_rat: Intermittent fault absent during the diagnostic window frustrates diagnosis; severity moderate — repeat visits and prolonged unresolved fault.
rows.sat_rat: Intermittent faults absent during the diagnostic window are a chronic, poorly-served problem. Waiting for an intermittent fault to appear wastes diagnostic time. Capturing intermittent faults demands high diagnostic skill and instrumentation. Missed intermittent faults cause repeat visits and cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of fill-finish disruption caused by restoration-related impacts on adjacent equipment
rows.plain: Shorten interruptions to filling and sealing products when nearby equipment is being restored or repaired. This helps keep production on schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Restoration impacts on adjacent equipment disrupting fill-finish is a spillover timeliness issue; severity moderate — production disruption.
rows.sat_rat: Adjacency impacts are inconsistently anticipated, so spillover disruption still occurs. Collateral impacts on adjacent equipment extend the effective outage duration unpredictably. Anticipating adjacency effects requires system-level expertise often missing at the bench. Adjacent disruption multiplies lost-production cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of relevant maintenance and operating history unavailable during mechanical failure diagnosis
rows.plain: Make more of the relevant repair and operating records available when a machine breaks down. This helps people find the true cause of the failure more quickly.
rows.imp_rat: Missing maintenance/operating history during diagnosis hampers root-cause work; severity moderate — slower or less accurate diagnosis.
rows.sat_rat: Relevant maintenance and operating history is often unavailable at diagnosis time, degrading conclusion quality. Hunting for missing history wastes diagnostic time, but the defect is the missing data itself. Interpreting sparse history takes skill, yet the core problem is information availability, not expertise. Missing history extends investigations and cost, but the primary loss is unreliable diagnosis inputs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of visible uncertainty about which competing explanation is the mechanical root cause
rows.plain: Make it clear which of several possible mechanical problems is the actual underlying cause. This helps people choose the right fix instead of guessing.
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty among competing root-cause explanations reflects unresolved diagnosis; severity moderate — risk of fixing the wrong cause.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a required replacement component is unavailable when repair begins
rows.plain: Make sure the needed replacement part is available before a repair starts. This helps prevent the repair from being delayed.
rows.imp_rat: Component unavailable when repair begins delays restoration; severity moderate — outage extension, a duplicate logistics concern.
rows.sat_rat: Required parts being unavailable at start is a common, disruptive occurrence. Missing parts at job start halts progress entirely. Little skill is involved; availability is a stocking issue. Stockouts drive expedite costs and extended downtime.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of discovering additional mechanical damage after disassembly begins
rows.plain: Find as much mechanical damage as possible before taking the equipment apart. This helps avoid unexpected repairs and longer stoppages later.
rows.imp_rat: Discovering added damage after disassembly is a scope-expansion nuisance; severity moderate — schedule and cost impact, manageable.
rows.sat_rat: Hidden damage regularly surfaces only after disassembly, a chronic uncertainty. Discovering hidden damage mid-job causes major time overruns. Anticipating latent damage before disassembly requires deep experience. Surprise damage means unplanned parts and extended outage costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of failed post-maintenance functional checks requiring repeat work before equipment handback
rows.plain: Reduce the number of equipment checks that fail after maintenance and require the work to be done again before the equipment is returned. This helps return equipment to service more quickly and reliably.
rows.imp_rat: Failed post-maintenance checks requiring repeat work is a rework/efficiency issue; severity moderate since failures are caught before handback.
rows.sat_rat: Failed post-maintenance checks recur at a meaningful rate requiring rework. Repeat work after a failed check directly extends equipment downtime. Reducing repeats depends on getting the repair right first time, a skill matter. Rework loops waste labor hours and consumables.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of restoration evidence lacking traceability to the evaluated equipment and completed service work
rows.plain: Make sure proof that equipment was restored is clearly linked to the specific equipment and service work that was completed. This matters because it shows that the right equipment was repaired and checked.
rows.imp_rat: Evidence lacking traceability to equipment/service is a documentation-integrity issue; severity moderate-high — GMP compliance and questionable release basis.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence frequently lacks solid links to the specific equipment and work, weakening confidence in conclusions. Establishing traceability adds documentation time but the error is broken linkage, not slowness. Maintaining rigorous traceability needs disciplined documentation practice more than rare expertise. Poor traceability triggers costly re-verification, but the primary failing is evidential integrity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of adjacent components damaged during disassembly or reassembly
rows.plain: Reduce the number of nearby parts that are damaged while equipment is taken apart or put back together. This matters because extra damage can lead to more repairs, delays, and costs.
rows.imp_rat: Adjacent components damaged during disassembly/reassembly is a collateral-damage defect; severity moderate — additional repair scope and risk.
rows.sat_rat: Adjacent-component damage occurs occasionally in tight assemblies. Protecting adjacent parts during disassembly adds handling time. Avoiding adjacent damage depends on careful technique and access planning. Collateral damage drives extra parts and labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of altering or damaging serviced components while obtaining mechanical measurements
rows.plain: Avoid changing or damaging repaired parts while taking measurements of them. This matters because the measurement should show their condition without creating a new problem.
rows.imp_rat: Damaging components while measuring is a self-inflicted defect during verification; severity moderate — introduces a defect during the check.
rows.sat_rat: Damaging parts while measuring is uncommon with standard tools. Careful measurement adds little time. Taking measurements without altering components requires technique and proper tools. Measurement-induced damage is rare and low cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that restoration work is authorized before acceptance-criteria approval
rows.plain: Do not allow repair or restoration work to begin until the approval rules have been reviewed and approved. This prevents work from being accepted without agreed standards.
rows.imp_rat: Authorizing work before acceptance-criteria approval is a governance/sequencing error; severity moderate-high — rework and possible non-conforming acceptance.
rows.sat_rat: Work-authorization controls exist, yet reactive repairs are sometimes started before criteria are formally approved, creating recurring gaps. Requiring acceptance-criteria approval before authorization inserts a gate that is usually manageable within scheduled work. Approvers with quality authority are designated, but consistent enforcement relies on their discipline. The approval step consumes reviewer time but no significant material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of equipment components damaged during scheduled maintenance
rows.plain: Prevent equipment parts from being damaged during planned servicing. This reduces repairs, delays, and the risk of equipment not working properly.
rows.imp_rat: Components damaged during maintenance is a self-inflicted defect; severity moderate — rework and potential integrity impact if undetected.
rows.sat_rat: Incidental damage during scheduled maintenance is uncommon but not eliminated. Careful handling adds modest time during scheduled work. Avoiding collateral damage relies on technician care and dexterity. Damaged components during PM add unplanned replacement cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that inspection activity damages or misaligns equipment components
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that checking the equipment damages its parts or moves them out of alignment. This helps keep the equipment working correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Inspection activity damaging/misaligning components is a self-inflicted defect during verification; severity moderate — new defect from the checking step.
rows.sat_rat: Inspection rarely damages components under normal practice. Careful inspection adds little time. Non-damaging inspection technique is a routine competence. Inspection-caused damage is infrequent and inexpensive when it happens.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of required repair parts being unavailable for scheduled integrity work
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that needed repair parts are unavailable when planned equipment checks or repairs are due. This helps prevent delays and keep the equipment working properly.
rows.imp_rat: Parts unavailable for scheduled integrity work delays planned restoration; severity moderate — deferred maintenance and schedule slip.
rows.sat_rat: Planned maintenance kitting means parts are usually available for scheduled integrity work, with occasional shortfalls. Parts for scheduled work are ordered in advance so lead time is manageable when planning is good. Kitting the right parts needs planning knowledge but is not the main constraint. Holding scheduled-work inventory costs money but planned demand makes it predictable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which concealed damage expands the repair scope after work begins
rows.plain: Find hidden damage before repairs begin so that it does not expand the amount of repair work later. This helps shorten the time equipment is out of service.
rows.imp_rat: Concealed damage expanding scope after work begins is a variability/planning issue; severity moderate — schedule and cost overrun.
rows.sat_rat: Hidden damage routinely expands scope because pre-repair diagnostics cannot fully see inside assemblies. Concealed damage discovered after opening extends the outage beyond plan. Predicting concealed damage needs diagnostic experience that few possess. Scope expansion drives unbudgeted cost repeatedly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of classifying equipment within required tolerances as out of tolerance
rows.plain: Avoid wrongly deciding that equipment is outside its allowed limits when it is actually acceptable. This prevents unnecessary repairs, delays, or replacement.
rows.imp_rat: Classifying in-tolerance equipment as out-of-tolerance is a false-reject causing unnecessary rework; severity moderate — cost and delay, no safety harm.
rows.sat_rat: Misclassifying in-tolerance as out is a moderate, controllable error. Re-checking suspected out-of-tolerance readings adds time. Avoiding false rejects requires measurement skill and correct reference limits. False out-of-tolerance calls waste good parts and effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of relevant lifecycle cost components omitted from restoration-option evaluations
rows.plain: Reduce the share of important costs left out when comparing ways to restore the equipment. This gives a more complete picture of what each option will cost over its full working life.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting lifecycle cost components from restoration evaluations is largely a financial/decision-support concern peripheral to restoring integrity; severity modest.
rows.sat_rat: Because templates and habits vary, cost coverage is inconsistent and important components are repeatedly left out. Assembling full lifecycle cost components takes analyst time, but the error is about omission rather than delay. Complete lifecycle costing requires financial-modeling expertise that maintenance recommenders often lack, so components get overlooked. Omitted cost components directly distort the money comparison between restoration options, which is the core loss this need embodies.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of equipment replacement risk omitted from lifecycle comparisons of restoration options
rows.plain: Reduce the risk of equipment replacement being left out when comparing ways to restore the equipment. This helps ensure the comparison reflects the possibility that replacement may eventually be needed.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting replacement risk from lifecycle comparisons is a decision-analysis gap peripheral to the physical restoration; severity modest.
rows.sat_rat: Restoration-versus-replace comparisons routinely omit replacement risk, so recommendations rest on an inconsistent, incomplete basis. The omission of replacement risk is not a speed issue but a completeness-of-comparison issue. Judging replacement risk against restoration demands specialized reliability-engineering insight that is scarce in typical evaluations. Missing replacement risk skews the economic case, but the primary defect is an incomplete, unreliable comparison.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of interventions receiving a replacement component that does not match the installed equipment configuration
rows.imp_rat: A replacement component mismatching configuration corrupts the restored equipment (relevance/severity), constrained under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Configuration mismatches in issued parts occur intermittently. Confirming part-configuration match adds a check. Matching part to installed configuration needs config knowledge. Wrong-config parts waste stock and force rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between verification conditions and production loads, temperatures, or motion
rows.imp_rat: Verification conditions mismatching production loads/temperatures/motion gives false integrity assurance (relevance/severity), capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Verification conditions frequently mismatch real production loads. Recreating production conditions for verification takes time. Judging condition equivalence requires process understanding. Off-condition verification lets defects through at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that signs of renewed mechanical degradation remain undetected after return to service
rows.imp_rat: Undetected renewed degradation after return to service risks recurrence and failure escape (relevance/severity), but ranking caps at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Post-restart monitoring is inconsistent, so re-emerging degradation is often caught late. Renewed degradation after restart can go undetected for long intervals between inspections. Early detection needs skilled monitoring interpretation that is scarce. Late detection escalates repair and quality cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminating a sterile production area while examining failed equipment
rows.imp_rat: Contaminating a sterile area while examining failed equipment risks product loss (relevance/severity), but capped at 3.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Established containment and gowning protocols make examination-driven area contamination relatively rare. Contamination during examination is a breach event, not a timing issue. Examining failed equipment without contaminating the area needs disciplined aseptic technique. An area contamination is very costly, but preventing the breach is the primary aim.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination being introduced while restoring sterile fill-finish equipment
rows.imp_rat: Contamination during sterile fill-finish restoration is highly relevant to the CFJ and catastrophic in consequence, but the batch ceiling caps this at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Mature aseptic protocols exist and are widely enforced, making contamination during restoration uncommon though never zero. Contamination control adds procedural steps but the error is a breach, not a delay. Aseptic restoration technique demands specialized, tightly controlled skill and vigilance. A contamination event is catastrophically costly, but the primary concern is preventing the breach.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that qualified mechanical resources are unavailable during approved restoration windows
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable qualified resources during restoration windows directly stalls the repair job (relevance) and delays production recovery (severity), but capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Planning and CMMS scheduling exist but qualified resources are still frequently unavailable when windows open. Resource unavailability during approved windows is fundamentally a scheduling and timing failure. The shortage is of qualified people, so skill availability is tightly bound to this need. Missed windows cause costly delays, but the dominant loss is time within the window.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency in lifecycle boundaries applied across restoration options
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent lifecycle boundaries across restoration options is an analytical/decision consistency concern far from restoring integrity; severity low.
rows.sat_rat: Lifecycle boundaries are applied inconsistently across options, making comparisons unreliable. Inconsistent lifecycle boundaries confuse comparisons rather than slow them. Applying consistent boundaries requires analytical discipline and standards knowledge. Boundary inconsistency distorts cost comparisons, but the essence is comparison consistency.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which verification conditions differ from sterile fill-finish service demands
rows.imp_rat: Verification conditions differing from sterile service demands weakens qualification validity (relevance/severity), constrained to the 3.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Verification conditions often diverge from real sterile service demands, weakening confidence in results. Matching verification to service demands is about representativeness, not duration. Designing verification that mirrors sterile fill-finish demands specialized process knowledge. Unrepresentative verification causes costly field failures, but the core is validity of the test.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of compromising the sterile boundary during mechanical restoration verification
rows.imp_rat: Compromising the sterile boundary during verification threatens aseptic integrity (relevance/severity), constrained under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Verification protocols with defined controls make sterile-boundary compromise during verification uncommon. Compromising the sterile boundary during verification is a breach, not a delay. Maintaining boundary integrity during verification requires specialized aseptic verification skill. A boundary breach is costly, but the dominant concern is integrity of the sterile barrier.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that maintenance effects on sterile-product protection are overlooked during scoping
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking maintenance effects on sterile-product protection during scoping risks contamination escape (relevance/severity), held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Sterile-product protection is inconsistently addressed during maintenance scoping. Considering sterile-product protection during scoping adds review effort. Overlooking sterile-product impact reflects a shortage of aseptic-process expertise in scoping. Scoping cost is minor next to the risk of an overlooked impact.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of restoration-affected functions and interfaces excluded from protocol scope
rows.imp_rat: Excluding restoration-affected functions from protocol scope leaves verification gaps (relevance/severity), but ranking caps at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Restoration-affected functions are still excluded from protocol scope at times. Including all affected functions in protocol scope extends drafting effort. Ensuring full function/interface coverage needs system-level expertise unevenly applied. Cost is authoring labor, not the dominant concern.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of the validated operating envelope not represented by specified test conditions
rows.imp_rat: Untested portions of the validated operating envelope leave gaps in verified integrity (relevance/severity), constrained under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Test conditions frequently under-represent the full validated envelope, a recurring qualification gap. Covering the full operating envelope with test conditions lengthens qualification test time. Defining representative envelope coverage demands process-engineering expertise that is scarce. Broader test conditions increase resource use but the felt loss is unrepresentative coverage.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that specified qualification activities compromise the equipment's sterile state
rows.imp_rat: Qualification activities compromising the sterile state directly endangers aseptic integrity (relevance/severity), held to the 3.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Established aseptic qualification practice generally protects the sterile state with occasional lapses. Planning qualification to protect the sterile state adds setup time. Preserving the sterile state during testing demands scarce aseptic technique expertise. Sterility-preserving measures add cost but the concern is the sterile state itself.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty that a maintenance-related contamination pathway was overlooked
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty that a contamination pathway was overlooked is a residual sterile-risk gap (relevance/severity), constrained under 3.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that maintenance effects on the sterile boundary or contamination controls remain unrecognized
rows.imp_rat: Unrecognized maintenance effects on the sterile boundary is a latent contamination hazard (relevance/severity), capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Unrecognized maintenance effects on sterile boundaries remain a serious recurring gap. Recognizing sterile-boundary effects adds analysis time during scoping. Detecting effects on the sterile boundary needs specialized aseptic expertise that is limited. The cost is analysis effort, not the driving loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of intervention-affected validated equipment functions omitted from assessment
rows.imp_rat: Omitting validated functions from assessment risks unverified return-to-service (relevance/severity), constrained under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Omissions from assessment scope persist despite structured impact-assessment practice. Assessing intervention-affected functions adds review time built into the assessment. Identifying all intervention-affected functions requires experienced judgement not always applied. Assessment cost is minor relative to the exposure from omissions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of maintenance-affected equipment functions or interfaces omitted from qualification scope
rows.imp_rat: Omitting affected functions from qualification scope risks releasing unverified equipment (relevance/severity), but the ranking caps it at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Interface omissions remain a recurring weakness in maintenance qualification scoping. Mapping affected functions and interfaces adds scoping effort within normal timelines. Recognizing affected interfaces requires system-level understanding that is unevenly available. The scoping cost is small next to the risk of an omitted interface.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of qualification evidence not corresponding to the restored equipment configuration
rows.imp_rat: Qualification evidence not matching the restored configuration invalidates verification (relevance/severity), capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence-to-configuration mismatches occasionally slip through but are generally controlled. Confirming evidence corresponds to the restored configuration adds reconciliation time. Linking evidence to configuration relies on documentation discipline more than scarce expertise. Reconciliation cost is minor relative to a mismatch risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in pass-or-fail thresholds for restored mechanical integrity
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous pass/fail thresholds for restored integrity undermines the acceptance decision (relevance/severity), held to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Ambiguous thresholds remain a common and consequential authorship defect. Writing unambiguous pass/fail thresholds adds authoring and review time. Defining crisp thresholds demands validation expertise that varies widely by author. The cost is authoring labor, not a resource-driven concern.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that qualification conditions exclude relevant production, cleaning, sterilization, thermal, pressure, or mechanical stresses
rows.imp_rat: Qualification excluding relevant stresses leaves integrity unproven under real conditions (relevance/severity), held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Relevant stresses are commonly excluded from qualification conditions, leaving a real gap. Including production, cleaning, sterilization and stress conditions multiplies test time. Knowing which stresses must be represented requires broad multi-domain expertise rarely held by one author. Comprehensive stress testing consumes resources though the dominant risk is exclusion.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that qualification criteria omit restoration-created aseptic-boundary, particulate, or lubricant hazards
rows.imp_rat: Qualification criteria omitting restoration-created aseptic/particulate/lubricant hazards leaves contamination risks unchecked (relevance/severity), capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Restoration-specific contamination hazards are frequently omitted from qualification criteria. Adding aseptic, particulate and lubricant hazard criteria extends protocol development. Recognizing restoration-created contamination hazards requires specialized contamination-control expertise. The scoping cost is minor versus the contamination risk exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical mechanical functions tested under conditions unrepresentative of sterile fill-finish use
rows.imp_rat: Testing critical functions under unrepresentative conditions gives false assurance (relevance/severity), capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Critical functions are often tested under simplified, unrepresentative conditions. Testing under representative sterile fill-finish conditions extends qualification duration. Replicating realistic use conditions requires specialized process knowledge that is limited. Realistic-condition testing raises cost but the concern is representativeness, not spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that restoration-related contamination-control impacts are omitted from evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Omitting restoration contamination-control impacts from evaluation risks unrecognized hazards (relevance/severity), held to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Contamination-control impacts are commonly under-evaluated in maintenance qualification. Evaluating contamination-control impacts adds assessment time to scoping. Assessing contamination-control impacts demands scarce microbiology and CCS expertise. Assessment cost is small compared with a missed contamination impact.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the restored equipment configuration differs from the validated configuration
rows.imp_rat: A restored configuration differing from validated one undoes the job's core aim (relevance/severity), but the ranking caps at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Change control and as-built checks make configuration mismatches fairly uncommon. Verifying restored configuration against validated baseline adds a check step. Confirming configuration equivalence requires attention to as-built detail rather than rare skill. Configuration verification is low-cost versus a mismatch escaping.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between qualification conditions and validated production conditions
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between qualification and validated production conditions weakens verification validity (relevance/severity), held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Condition mismatches remain a recurring source of qualification weakness. Aligning qualification to production conditions adds setup and test time. Matching qualification to validated production conditions needs process expertise unevenly applied. The alignment cost is modest relative to the risk of mismatch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that qualification activities compromise the equipment's contamination-controlled state
rows.imp_rat: Qualification activities compromising the contamination-controlled state directly threatens sterility (relevance/severity), but capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard cleanroom and gowning protocols usually protect the controlled state during qualification. Designing qualification to preserve the contamination-controlled state adds planning time. Avoiding qualification-induced contamination requires specialized aseptic execution expertise. The planning cost is minor versus a compromised controlled state.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sterile-product impact is overlooked during disposition
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking sterile-product impact during disposition risks releasing hazardous equipment (relevance/severity), but capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Sterile-product impact is inconsistently weighed during disposition decisions. Considering sterile-product impact at disposition adds review time. Recognizing sterile-product impact at disposition requires aseptic expertise not always at the table. The review cost is small compared to the risk of an overlooked impact.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between the restored equipment configuration and the approved qualified configuration
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between restored and approved qualified configuration invalidates the release (relevance/severity), but ranking caps at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Configuration mismatches against the qualified state still occur where documentation is incomplete. Configuration verification against qualified state adds comparison time. Comparing to qualified configuration needs engineering knowledge that is available. Review-labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of mechanical defects unresolved at qualification completion
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved mechanical defects at qualification completion means the job isn't truly done (relevance/severity), capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Defect tracking usually catches issues, but some unresolved defects reach qualification completion. Resolving mechanical defects before qualification completion can delay closeout. Defect resolution depends on skilled mechanics and engineers who are available. Repair labor and parts cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of compromised sterile-boundary integrity following equipment restoration
rows.imp_rat: Compromised sterile-boundary integrity following restoration is a catastrophic contamination risk (relevance/severity), held to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Compromised sterile-boundary integrity following restoration is an infrequent but high-consequence recurring concern. Verifying sterile-boundary integrity after restoration adds testing time. Requires specialized integrity-testing expertise. Integrity testing carries cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of intended production conditions omitted from qualification testing
rows.imp_rat: Omitting intended production conditions from qualification testing leaves gaps in verified integrity (relevance/severity), capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Intended production conditions are sometimes omitted from qualification testing, leaving residual risk. Covering all production conditions in testing extends qualification time. Requires understanding of process conditions that specialists provide. Testing-labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterile-boundary and contamination-control requirements omitted from equipment acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Omitting sterile-boundary requirements from acceptance criteria risks contamination escapes (relevance/severity), but capped at 3.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Contamination-control requirements are often under-specified in mechanical acceptance criteria, a notable recurring gap. Adding contamination-control requirements lengthens criteria development. Sterile-boundary expertise is specialized and not always embedded in mechanical criteria authoring. Mostly specialist-review cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of restoration-related failure modes omitted from equipment acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Omitted failure modes from acceptance criteria can pass defective repairs (relevance/severity), but the batch ceiling caps at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Failure modes are commonly missed from acceptance criteria because FMEA rigor is inconsistently applied to restoration. Enumerating failure modes is time-consuming and often shortcut under pressure. Comprehensive failure-mode coverage depends on scarce, experienced reliability engineers. Largely an analysis-labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of equipment acceptance criteria lacking objective pass-or-fail thresholds
rows.imp_rat: Missing objective acceptance thresholds undermines valid repair verification (relevance) and can allow bad releases (severity), but capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Many acceptance criteria still rely on subjective wording without hard thresholds, a persistent recurring weakness. Defining objective thresholds up front takes effort but avoids later disputes. Setting measurable pass/fail thresholds requires engineering judgement that is not uniformly applied. Primarily a documentation-labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical equipment components and interfaces excluded from the acceptance boundary
rows.imp_rat: Excluding critical components/interfaces from the acceptance boundary leaves integrity unverified (relevance/severity), but ranking caps at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Critical components and interfaces are still sometimes excluded from the acceptance boundary. Defining the acceptance boundary thoroughly takes time. Identifying all critical components and interfaces needs engineering expertise. Analysis-labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of acceptance criteria that cannot be conclusively evaluated under approved restoration conditions
rows.imp_rat: Criteria that cannot be conclusively evaluated undermines the release decision (relevance/severity), but capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Criteria that cannot be conclusively evaluated under approved conditions still slip through and surface late. Confirming criteria are testable under approved conditions can delay approval. Judging evaluability under real restoration conditions needs experienced reviewers. Primarily review-labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of affected equipment components and interfaces excluded from the restoration scope
rows.imp_rat: Excluding affected components/interfaces from restoration scope leaves the job incomplete (relevance/severity), constrained under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Affected components and interfaces are occasionally left out of restoration scope, causing downstream gaps. Scoping all affected components adds planning time. Complete scope definition depends on experienced planners. Planning-labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that mechanical restoration compromises sterile-area contamination controls
rows.imp_rat: Mechanical restoration compromising sterile-area contamination controls directly threatens sterility (relevance/severity), but capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Contamination-control breaches during mechanical work in sterile areas remain a serious residual risk. Contamination-control verification adds steps to sterile-area work. Sterile-area intervention requires specialized aseptic training. Gowning, cleaning and verification carry real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of assigned service personnel whose required qualifications are not demonstrated before work begins
rows.imp_rat: Unproven qualifications before work begins risks defective repairs and compliance issues (relevance/severity), capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Qualification-before-work checks are common but not always enforced for reactive or contractor work. Pre-work qualification checks add a brief gate before work begins. This need centers on demonstrating personnel competence, supported by training and certification programs. Training and record upkeep costs are budgeted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination being introduced into sterile fill-finish equipment during restoration
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contamination into sterile fill-finish equipment during restoration is a core sterile-risk error (relevance/severity), capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Contamination introduced into sterile fill-finish equipment during restoration remains a persistent high-severity risk. Contamination prevention procedures extend intervention time. Aseptic-intervention skill is specialized and scarce. Cleaning and requalification costs are significant.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of personnel performing or approving restoration without required qualification or authorization
rows.imp_rat: Unqualified personnel performing/approving restoration threatens work integrity and compliance (relevance/severity), but ranking caps at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Authorization matrices reduce unqualified sign-offs, but lapsed or unverified qualifications occasionally go unchecked. Qualification verification is a quick lookup when training records are current. The need is fundamentally about ensuring personnel hold required qualifications, and training-record systems support this. Maintaining qualification records has administrative cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required replacement components unavailable for use because quality status or traceability cannot be verified
rows.imp_rat: Unverifiable component quality status blocks installation (relevance) and risks noncompliant parts (severity), held to the 3.0 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Parts blocked by unverifiable quality status are a recurring nuisance. Verifying quality status can delay use of parts. Checking traceability and status is a procedural competence. Unusable parts due to unverifiable status waste stock value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of reactive repair delay caused by unavailable approved replacement components
rows.imp_rat: Delay from unavailable approved components hampers reactive repair timing (relevance) but consequences are recoverable downtime (severity), held under the 3.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Part-driven repair delays are frequent and disruptive. Waiting for unavailable approved parts directly extends reactive downtime. No special skill mitigates this; it is a supply issue. Extended downtime and expedites raise cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of physical fault evidence altered before maintenance assessment
rows.imp_rat: Altered physical fault evidence can misdirect root-cause and repair (severity) but is upstream of restore execution (relevance); held under ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Physical fault evidence is often disturbed before assessment, a common problem. Preserving fault evidence competes with restart pressure. Knowing to preserve evidence before disturbing it requires discipline. Altered evidence forces costlier investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between the consequence of an unresolved mechanical condition and its assigned urgency
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between condition consequence and assigned urgency can delay critical action (severity), a prioritization issue (relevance); held under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Urgency assignments frequently mismatch actual consequence. Aligning urgency to consequence takes assessment time. Judging consequence-to-urgency mapping requires risk understanding. Mismatched urgency causes over- or under-spend on response.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of inspection findings recorded with an inaccurate condition or component identity
rows.imp_rat: Inaccurate inspection findings can misdirect the repair (relevance) and cause wrong dispositions (severity), but stays within the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Inaccurate condition or identity in findings still occurs at a moderate rate. Accurate recording of findings adds modest documentation time. Correctly identifying condition and component needs competence. Inaccurate findings cause wrong follow-up spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between recorded and observed as-found or as-left equipment conditions
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between recorded and observed as-found/as-left conditions corrupts the record of integrity (severity), a data-fidelity issue (relevance); under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual, inconsistent recording means recorded and observed conditions often diverge in current practice. Discrepancies surface late and force re-inspection that adds time before work can proceed. Accurate as-found/as-left capture depends on the diligence and skill of the individual technician. Missing condition data drives repeat visits and misordered parts that waste resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of production equipment assigned mechanical criticality inconsistent with production and patient-risk consequences
rows.imp_rat: Mis-assigned criticality is a prioritization error somewhat upstream of the repair act (relevance) but can misallocate risk attention (severity); under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Criticality-ranking methods exist but assignments frequently drift out of alignment with actual consequences. Criticality assessment is a periodic exercise, not a time-pressured one. Assigning criticality consistent with patient and production risk requires cross-disciplinary judgement. Criticality analysis costs engineering effort but is a modest one-time investment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of proficiency in infrequent critical repairs degrading below the required standard between uses
rows.imp_rat: Degraded proficiency in infrequent critical repairs risks execution errors on high-stakes work (severity) but is a latent competency issue (relevance); under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Few sites systematically refresh rarely-used critical skills, so proficiency degradation between uses is poorly controlled. Skill decay happens over time between uses, but the loss is competence not delay. Infrequently performed critical repairs cause proficiency to degrade, a core skill-retention problem. Refresher drills and simulations cost money and are often skipped.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between identification of a high-risk equipment condition and escalation to accountable decision-makers
rows.imp_rat: Delay escalating a high-risk condition can let hazards persist (severity) but is a communication timing issue (relevance); held under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Escalation pathways exist but are inconsistently triggered and timed. Escalating a high-risk condition to decision-makers can be slow through informal channels. Recognizing escalation-worthy conditions requires judgement that varies. Delayed escalation lets avoidable failure costs accrue.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that approved recurrence-control actions remain incomplete after equipment returns to service
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete recurrence-control actions after return-to-service leave failure risk open (severity) but is a follow-up delay (relevance); under ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: CAPA tracking exists but completion routinely lags, leaving controls incomplete. Recurrence-control actions often stay open long after equipment is back in service. Closing actions well requires engineering follow-through that competes for time. Open actions leave residual failure-cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between completion of mechanical restoration and authorization to restart production
rows.imp_rat: Delay between restoration completion and restart authorization affects throughput timing (relevance) with recoverable cost (severity), held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Authorization timing is inconsistent depending on approver availability and paperwork. The gap between repair completion and restart authorization is a recurring administrative delay. Restart decisions need qualified approvers whose availability limits speed. Every hour of authorization lag is lost production value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of production interruption beyond the approved mechanical restoration window
rows.imp_rat: Production interruption beyond the approved window is a schedule overrun central to timely restore (relevance) with recoverable cost (severity), at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Overruns beyond approved windows occur regularly despite planning, reflecting inconsistent execution. This need is explicitly about the duration of interruption exceeding the approved window. Staying within the window depends on skilled execution, but the measured loss is elapsed time. Overrun time carries production cost, yet the dominant concern is the duration itself.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the maintenance intervention receives a lower GMP risk class than warranted
rows.imp_rat: Assigning a too-low GMP risk class undercuts controls (severity), an upstream classification error to the physical repair (relevance); under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Under-classification of interventions persists despite risk-assessment procedures. Assigning risk class is a quick step within change control. Assigning an adequate GMP risk class relies on experienced risk judgement that is inconsistent. The classification effort is negligible in cost terms.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required equipment-status and environmental prerequisites omitted from the protocol
rows.imp_rat: Omitted equipment-status/environmental prerequisites from the protocol can invalidate qualification (severity), a protocol-completeness issue (relevance); under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Prerequisite omissions from protocols are a recurring but usually caught issue. Listing status and environmental prerequisites adds protocol authoring time. Identifying required prerequisites relies on execution experience that varies by author. The authoring cost is small versus the risk of an omitted prerequisite.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the change classification understates the intervention's effect on validated status
rows.imp_rat: Understated change classification can bypass needed qualification (severity) but is a classification-judgment issue (relevance); held under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Change classification understating impact is a known recurring risk in change control. Reviewing change classification adds a checkpoint that rarely extends timelines. Correct classification depends heavily on QA judgement that varies between reviewers. Classification review is low-cost versus the risk of understatement.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of validation requirements lacking traceable links to assessed maintenance effects
rows.imp_rat: Validation requirements lacking traceable links to maintenance effects weakens verification linkage (severity), largely documentary (relevance); under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Missing traceable links between requirements and effects are a recurring documentation gap. Linking validation requirements to assessed effects adds traceability work. Building these traceable links requires disciplined validation practice more than rare skill. Traceability effort is inexpensive versus audit exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of qualification criteria lacking traceability to approved limits or product requirements
rows.imp_rat: Qualification criteria lacking traceability to approved limits weakens defensibility (severity), documentary to the repair (relevance); capped under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Criteria lacking traceability to approved limits are a frequent audit finding. Establishing traceability to approved limits adds documentation effort. Building criteria traceability requires disciplined validation practice more than rare skill. Traceability effort is inexpensive versus audit exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of acceptance criteria lacking traceability to restoration impacts and validation requirements
rows.imp_rat: Acceptance criteria lacking traceability to restoration impacts weakens verification linkage (severity), largely documentary (relevance); under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Untraceable acceptance criteria persist as a recurring documentation gap. Tracing criteria to restoration impacts adds review time within protocol drafting. Linking acceptance criteria to restoration impacts needs analytical expertise unevenly applied. The cost is documentation labor, not the dominant loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unexplained deviations or anomalous results in the restoration evidence
rows.imp_rat: Unexplained deviations/anomalies in restoration evidence signal possible undetected defects (severity), an evidence-quality issue (relevance); under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Unexplained anomalies in restoration evidence still occur despite deviation processes. Investigating anomalous results adds investigation time to the evidence cycle. Explaining anomalies requires strong root-cause expertise that is unevenly available. Investigation cost is modest versus the risk of unexplained results.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of qualification deviations unresolved when validated status is declared
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved deviations when validated status is declared risks false validation (severity), a closeout-discipline issue (relevance); under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Formal deviation-management systems make declaring status with open deviations uncommon. Closing all deviations before declaring validated status can extend timelines. Resolving deviations properly requires QA investigation expertise that is limited. Deviation closure consumes labor but the loss is unresolved status, not spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of qualification results derived from instruments with invalid calibration status
rows.imp_rat: Qualification results from invalid-calibration instruments give false verification (severity), a data-integrity issue to the restore (relevance); under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration-management systems reliably flag out-of-cal instruments, so this need is well controlled with only rare lapses. Calibration-status verification is a quick check with modern systems. Calibration management is a well-established discipline with clear procedures. Calibration programs are a standard budgeted cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of qualification evidence lacking traceability to restoration work and source data
rows.imp_rat: Qualification evidence lacking traceability to source data weakens defensibility (severity), largely documentary (relevance); capped under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence traceability to source data is frequently incomplete, a persistent inspection finding. Building traceability links between evidence and work adds documentation time. Establishing traceability requires disciplined record-keeping practice. Documentation-labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of restoration-related changes lacking approved quality disposition before qualification approval
rows.imp_rat: Changes lacking quality disposition before qualification approval risk noncompliant release (severity) though administrative to the repair (relevance); under ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Disposition gates exist but changes sometimes reach qualification approval without complete quality disposition. Securing quality disposition before qualification approval adds review cycles. Quality dispositioners are designated and experienced. Review-labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of qualification tests executed outside the approved protocol
rows.imp_rat: Tests executed outside the approved protocol invalidate qualification (severity) but is an execution-compliance issue (relevance); under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Protocol-driven qualification limits deviations, but off-protocol execution still happens occasionally. Protocol adherence adds structured steps but is planned. Executing to approved protocol needs trained qualification staff. Labor cost of protocol execution.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of acceptance criteria based on inaccurate equipment-condition information
rows.imp_rat: Acceptance criteria based on inaccurate condition info risks wrong release decisions (severity), a data-input issue (relevance); capped under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Criteria built on inaccurate condition information still occur where inspection data is stale or incomplete. Verifying equipment-condition data adds time. Requires accurate condition assessment skill. Assessment-labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required restoration records and acceptance evidence elements omitted from equipment acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Missing acceptance-evidence elements is largely documentary to the physical restore job (relevance) though it can block release (severity); under ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Required records and evidence elements are frequently omitted from criteria, driving downstream documentation gaps. Assembling the full list of required records adds preparation time. Knowing which evidence elements are mandatory requires documented-standards knowledge that varies by author. Modest administrative cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of restoration records lacking traceability among the affected component, approved scope, performed work, and acceptance decision
rows.imp_rat: Restoration records lacking end-to-end traceability weakens defensibility and audit (severity), documentary to the repair (relevance); under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: End-to-end record traceability is commonly broken, making this a chronic weak area. Linking records across component, scope, work and decision is time-intensive. Requires meticulous documentation discipline often lacking. Documentation-labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of restoration records containing unexplained changes, noncontemporaneous entries, or unattributed approvals
rows.imp_rat: Unexplained/noncontemporaneous/unattributed record entries are data-integrity failures with compliance stakes (severity), documentary to the job (relevance); under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Unexplained changes and unattributed approvals persist where records are not electronically controlled, a recurring integrity gap. Investigating unexplained changes consumes time. Detecting noncontemporaneous or unattributed entries needs data-integrity expertise. Investigation-labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that documented restoration status conflicts with the actual equipment configuration or release status
rows.imp_rat: Documented status conflicting with actual configuration risks operating on false release info (severity), a records-truth issue (relevance); capped under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Documented status still conflicts with actual configuration or release status more often than desired. Reconciling documented status with actual state adds verification time. Requires careful cross-checking discipline. Verification-labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of restoration deviations lacking documented disposition or approval before equipment release
rows.imp_rat: Undispositioned deviations before release risk noncompliant equipment reaching production (severity), documentary to the repair (relevance); under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation-management systems capture most cases, though undocumented dispositions still occasionally precede release. Deviation disposition before release can extend the release timeline. Deviation handling requires quality expertise that is normally staffed. Investigation labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of restoration acceptance decisions based on instruments with invalid calibration status when used
rows.imp_rat: Acceptance decisions on invalid-calibration instruments risk bad releases (severity), instrument-dependency issue (relevance); capped under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration controls make decisions on invalid instruments uncommon, though field use of uncalibrated tools occasionally occurs. Checking instrument status before an acceptance decision is fast. Standard calibration procedures require little special judgement. Covered by existing calibration programs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the restoration qualification impact is underestimated during evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Underestimating qualification impact risks insufficient verification (severity), an assessment-judgment issue to the restore (relevance); under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Underestimated qualification impact is a common judgement failure that leads to inadequate requalification. Impact assessment adds evaluation time before recommendation. Correctly judging qualification impact demands scarce, experienced assessors and is easy to underestimate. Assessment-labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between the restored equipment configuration and the approved baseline
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch to the approved baseline drives rework and invalid state (severity) though partly a documentation-alignment issue (relevance); under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Baseline mismatches persist where as-built records are not kept current. Baseline comparison adds verification steps. Requires accurate baseline records and engineering judgement. Review-labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of applicable GMP requirements omitted from restoration evaluation criteria
rows.imp_rat: Omitted GMP requirements from evaluation risks compliance failures (severity) though somewhat administrative to the physical restore (relevance); under ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: GMP coverage checklists help, yet applicable requirements are still occasionally omitted from restoration evaluation. Mapping GMP requirements into criteria adds review effort. Requires GMP interpretation skill that quality specialists supply but is not always fully applied. Review-labor cost.
