rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of restoration tasks performed outside approved instructions or acceptance limits
rows.imp_rat: Restoration tasks outside approved instructions risk defective/noncompliant work (severity), an execution-discipline issue (relevance); under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Instruction adherence is generally good but tasks performed outside approved limits still recur, especially in reactive work. Following approved instructions is routine and does not add material delay. Work instructions reduce reliance on individual skill. Minimal added cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of restoration records containing missing, inaccurate, or nontraceable entries
rows.imp_rat: Records with missing/inaccurate entries undermine traceability and release integrity (severity), documentary to the repair (relevance); under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Missing, inaccurate, or nontraceable entries remain a frequent data-integrity problem in paper-heavy records. Correcting record errors adds rework time. Accurate, complete entries depend on operator attentiveness. Rework-labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required calibration, inspection, or qualification checks incomplete at equipment release
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete calibration/inspection checks at release leave integrity unproven (severity) though a closeout gap (relevance); capped under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Release checklists reliably catch most incomplete checks, though gaps recur under time pressure. Completing calibration and inspection checks before release adds scheduled steps. Technicians and calibration staff are qualified for these checks. Calibration and inspection resources are budgeted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of installed parts or materials failing approved identity or suitability specifications
rows.imp_rat: Installed parts failing identity/suitability specs corrupt the restored equipment (severity), a material-control issue (relevance); capped under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Approved-parts controls catch most nonconforming items, though suitability failures occasionally slip through. Verifying part identity adds a receiving/installation check. Parts verification is a defined procedure needing modest skill. Covered by parts-control processes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of discrepancy between documented restoration results and the actual as-left equipment condition
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between documented results and actual as-left condition undermines trust in the release (severity), a data-fidelity issue (relevance); under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Discrepancies between documented results and actual as-left condition recur where verification is weak. Verifying documented results against as-left condition adds effort. Needs attentive as-left verification practice. Verification-labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required quality dispositions unresolved at equipment release
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved quality dispositions at release risk premature return-to-service (severity), somewhat administrative to the physical job (relevance); under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Release checklists flag unresolved dispositions, but some still slip through under schedule pressure. Resolving open dispositions before release is a common bottleneck. Requires quality-review judgement that is available. Labor cost of resolution.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of spare-part records whose stated quantity or status differs from physically usable stock
rows.imp_rat: Spare-part records differing from usable stock disrupts planning (relevance) with moderate consequence (severity), under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Spare-part record accuracy is commonly poor against physical stock. Record-vs-stock mismatches trigger time-consuming reconciliation. Not skill-limited; it is inventory data hygiene. Inaccurate records cause stockouts and over-ordering costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent locating a replacement component absent from its recorded location
rows.imp_rat: Time locating a component absent from its recorded location delays repair (relevance) with recoverable cost (severity), below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Parts absent from recorded locations are a frequent time sink. Searching for misplaced parts wastes significant time. No special skill; depends on inventory accuracy. Search time and duplicate ordering add cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of stocked replacement components unusable because of damage, degradation, corrosion, or expiration
rows.imp_rat: Stocked components unusable from damage/expiration cause delays and waste (relevance) with recoverable impact (severity), below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Some stocked parts turn out unusable when needed. Discovering unusable stock delays a job needing that part. Detecting degradation needs basic inspection. Degraded, expired or corroded stock is direct wasted inventory value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration an on-hand replacement component is delayed between request and arrival at the repair location
rows.imp_rat: Delay of an on-hand component between request and arrival slows repair (relevance) with recoverable schedule cost (severity), under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: On-hand part delivery to the repair site is moderately reliable but variable. Delay between request and delivery of on-hand parts extends jobs. No skill dependency; it is logistics. Delivery delays extend downtime cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which a reserved replacement component is reassigned to a competing repair before scheduled maintenance
rows.imp_rat: A reserved component reassigned before scheduled maintenance disrupts planning (relevance) with recoverable delay impact (severity), below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Reserved parts are sometimes poached by competing repairs. Reassigned reserved parts force re-sourcing delays. Not skill-related; a reservation-control issue. Reassignment causes expedite costs and rescheduling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of interventions delayed because required companion seals, fasteners, or fittings are missing
rows.imp_rat: Missing companion seals/fasteners delays interventions (relevance) with recoverable schedule consequences (severity), comfortably below 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Interventions are often delayed for want of small companion parts. Missing companion seals or fasteners stall the job mid-stream. Anticipating needed consumables is a planning competence. Delays and expedited small parts add cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of component-removal delay caused by seized or inaccessible mechanical joints
rows.imp_rat: Seized-joint removal delay slows a repair task (relevance) with modest recoverable schedule impact (severity), well within the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Seized or inaccessible joints routinely delay component removal. Seized joints cause substantial unplanned removal delays. Freeing seized joints demands technique and patience. Extended teardown and potential damage add cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of equipment unavailability attributable to post-repair functional and mechanical checks
rows.imp_rat: Unavailability from post-repair checks extends downtime (relevance) with recoverable cost (severity), below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Check-driven unavailability is predictable but sizable. Post-repair checks keep equipment unavailable, extending downtime. Efficient checking depends on competence but is routine. Extended unavailability carries production opportunity cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of discrepancy between recorded mechanical measurements and observations made during the intervention
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between recorded and observed measurements is a data-fidelity issue (relevance) with moderate downstream risk (severity), under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Record-vs-observation discrepancies persist at a moderate rate. Reconciling records with observations adds documentation effort. Accurate transcription of measurements requires care. Discrepancies drive investigation costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of disagreement among repeated readings of the same mechanical condition under unchanged conditions
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among repeated readings is a measurement-repeatability issue (relevance) with moderate decision risk (severity), below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Repeatability of readings is moderate, dependent on technique and tools. Repeat readings to confirm consistency add time. Consistent measurement technique reduces reading scatter. Inconsistent readings cause rework and re-measurement cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of conflict among approved mechanical acceptance limits applicable to serviced equipment
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting acceptance limits create ambiguity in judging integrity (relevance) with moderate decision-risk (severity), under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Conflicts among acceptance limits arise occasionally and slow decisions. Reconciling conflicting acceptance limits adds decision time. Interpreting conflicting limits requires standards knowledge. Conflicting limits cause wrong accept/reject decisions at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of waiting for serviced equipment to reach required temperature and load conditions for valid tolerance checks
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for temperature/load conditions for valid tolerance checks delays verification (relevance) with recoverable impact (severity), below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Warm-up and load-condition waits before valid checks are common. Waiting for equipment to reach temperature/load for valid checks adds delay. Not skill-limited; a thermal/process constraint. Wait time extends outage cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency among operator observations, alarm chronology, and equipment condition at the fault event
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency among operator observations and alarm chronology hampers fault diagnosis (relevance) with moderate consequence (severity), under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Observation, alarm and condition accounts frequently disagree, hampering diagnosis. Reconciling operator accounts and alarm logs takes investigative time. Correlating disparate evidence demands analytical skill. Inconsistent evidence prolongs diagnosis at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of repeated disassembly and reassembly of unaffected equipment assemblies during diagnosis
rows.imp_rat: Repeated disassembly of unaffected assemblies wastes diagnostic effort (relevance) with recoverable rework cost (severity), below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Repeated disassembly of unaffected assemblies happens when diagnosis is weak. Unnecessary teardown of unaffected assemblies wastes diagnostic time. Targeted diagnosis to avoid needless disassembly requires expertise. Extra disassembly wears parts and adds labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between recognizing an out-of-scope mechanical condition and notifying the accountable party
rows.imp_rat: Delay notifying the accountable party of out-of-scope conditions slows response (relevance) with moderate impact (severity), below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Notification of out-of-scope conditions is sometimes slow but generally happens. Delay from recognition to notification extends resolution time. Prompt notification is procedural, not skill-limited. Notification delay prolongs downtime cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of escalations missing the affected location, observed symptoms, completed work, or current equipment state
rows.imp_rat: Escalations missing key context slows response (relevance) with moderate delay consequence (severity), under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Escalations often omit key details, requiring follow-up. Assembling complete escalation details adds brief effort. Providing full location/symptom/state info requires diligence. Incomplete escalations cause back-and-forth and delay cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an unresolved mechanical condition is escalated to a party without authority to determine the next action
rows.imp_rat: Escalating to a party without authority stalls resolution (relevance) with moderate delay consequence (severity), below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Escalations sometimes go to parties lacking authority to decide. Routing to the right authority can add a step but saves rework. Knowing who has authority requires organizational knowledge. Misdirected escalations waste time and effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration an escalated mechanical condition remains without an acknowledged owner
rows.imp_rat: An escalated condition without acknowledged owner lingers unresolved (relevance) with moderate consequence (severity), under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Escalated conditions often linger without an acknowledged owner. Escalations without an owner sit idle, extending downtime. Little skill involved; ownership assignment is a process gap. Orphaned escalations prolong costly outages.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of disagreement among applicable criteria for classifying observed mechanical deterioration
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among deterioration-classification criteria is an ambiguity that muddies judgment (relevance) with moderate consequence (severity), below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Criteria for classifying deterioration are inconsistently applied. Resolving classification disagreements adds time. Applying deterioration criteria consistently needs judgement. Misclassification drives unnecessary or missed repairs at cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which reactive repair demand exceeds available labor capacity during a production shift
rows.imp_rat: Reactive demand exceeding labor capacity during a shift strains resourcing (relevance) with moderate throughput consequence (severity), under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: There is no reliable buffer for reactive surges, so capacity overruns are a frequent, poorly mitigated event. Reactive spikes routinely overwhelm the shift and force critical repairs to queue, directly extending downtime. Triaging overload demands seasoned dispatcher judgement to reshuffle labor on the fly. Overload forces overtime or deferral, both of which raise cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement between maintenance and production on repair priorities when labor is constrained
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement on repair priorities under constrained labor is a coordination friction (relevance) with moderate scheduling consequence (severity), below 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: With no shared prioritization framework, outcomes swing case by case and disagreement is a chronic recurring pain. Priority disputes between maintenance and production stall dispatch decisions while escalations play out. Resolving these conflicts leans on senior schedulers' negotiation experience rather than any shared rule. Unresolved priority tug-of-war leaves labor idle or misapplied, wasting constrained capacity.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between planned craft capabilities and repair requirements
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between planned craft capabilities and repair requirements is a resourcing gap (relevance) with moderate consequence (severity), below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Planned capability versus real requirement is inconsistently checked, so mismatches surface regularly. Craft-capability gaps found at execution stall the repair until qualified crew arrives. Matching craft capabilities to requirements depends on planner expertise with no systematic competency map. Capability mismatches produce abortive dispatches that waste mobilization cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty in maintenance task boundaries when effort is estimated
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty in task boundaries during estimation is a planning-ambiguity issue (relevance) with moderate consequence (severity), under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Task boundaries are defined ad hoc, so estimation inputs vary widely across similar jobs. Fuzzy task boundaries make estimates unstable, causing schedule slippage. Bounding a task correctly requires experienced estimators who know the equipment. Poorly bounded tasks are over- or under-resourced, wasting labor allocation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between assumed and required maintenance skill mix
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between assumed and required skill mix is a resourcing-planning gap (relevance) with moderate consequence (severity), under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Assumed skill mix is rarely verified against actual task needs, so mismatches recur inconsistently. Skill-mix mismatches discovered at the job cause delays while the right craft is found. The status quo relies on planners' tacit knowledge of who can do what, which is error-prone for uncommon tasks. Wrong skill assignment wastes labor hours and can force costly re-dispatch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between reported maintenance progress and completed work
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between reported progress and completed work misleads planning (relevance) with moderate consequence (severity), under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Progress reporting today is self-reported and coarse, so reported and actual completion routinely disagree. Overstated progress hides delays until they cascade, costing recovery time later. Judging true completion versus reported status requires supervisory experience to catch optimistic reporting. False progress signals cause premature resource reallocation and wasted mobilization.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of comparing maintenance progress with a superseded schedule
rows.imp_rat: Comparing progress with a superseded schedule misleads planning (relevance) with moderate consequence (severity), under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Schedule version control is weak in typical practice, so working from superseded schedules happens with some regularity. Comparing against a stale schedule wastes time chasing phantom deviations. Spotting that a schedule is superseded relies on individual alertness rather than system enforcement. Acting on an old schedule misallocates labor and effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of scheduled integrity tasks completed after their required date
rows.imp_rat: Integrity tasks completed after their required date slips compliance timing (relevance) with moderate consequence (severity), under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS scheduling tracks due dates well but overdue PM completion remains a common backlog problem. This is directly about tasks completed after their required date — a schedule-adherence timing metric. Skill is rarely the reason tasks slip; access and resource contention are. Late completion can incur compliance and failure costs but is not primarily a cost issue.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of scheduled maintenance tasks deferred because equipment is unavailable for service
rows.imp_rat: Deferred scheduled tasks from equipment unavailability slips maintenance timing (relevance) with recoverable consequence (severity), below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Maintenance scheduling coordinates with production but equipment-availability conflicts still routinely force deferrals. Deferrals hinge on securing production-access windows, fundamentally a scheduling/time-access problem. Little specialized skill is involved; the issue is access coordination. Deferred tasks raise later failure and cost risk but the driver is availability.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from equipment failure to authorization of reactive repair work
rows.imp_rat: Time from failure to reactive-repair authorization delays restoring integrity (relevance) with recoverable cost (severity), below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Reactive work-authorization workflows exist but approval delays in off-hours remain common. Explicitly about the time from failure to authorization of reactive repair. Authorization is procedural, requiring little specialized skill. Delayed authorization extends downtime cost but the metric is time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of reliability targets being misaligned with current approved operating requirements
rows.imp_rat: Reliability targets misaligned with operating requirements is a strategy-setting gap (relevance) with moderate consequence (severity), under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Reliability targets are set but frequently drift from current approved operating requirements. Realigning targets lags operating changes but timing is secondary to correctness. Aligning reliability targets with approved operating requirements needs engineering judgement. Misaligned targets can drive wrong spend but cost is indirect.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of maintenance commitments exceeding approved resources or production access
rows.imp_rat: Maintenance commitments exceeding approved resources is a planning-reliability issue (relevance) with moderate consequence (severity), under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Planning and scheduling tools help balance commitments but overcommitment against resources still occurs regularly. Overcommitment can cause delays but the error is committing beyond available resources. Resource/access planning is a management skill but not scarce expertise. The need is fundamentally about not exceeding approved resources and production access.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in criteria for selecting scheduled versus reactive repair
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous scheduled-vs-reactive selection criteria muddies strategy (relevance) with moderate consequence (severity), below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Selection criteria are often informal, leaving meaningful ambiguity in scheduled-versus-reactive decisions. Ambiguity slows decisions but the error is the decision ambiguity itself. Resolving scheduled-vs-reactive choices relies on judgement absent clear criteria. Wrong choices waste resources but that is downstream.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of maintenance-strategy misalignment after equipment or failure-evidence changes
rows.imp_rat: Maintenance-strategy misalignment after evidence changes is a stale-strategy gap (relevance) with moderate consequence (severity), under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Strategy updates after equipment or evidence changes are typically slow and reactive, leaving long misalignment windows. The need is explicitly the duration strategy stays misaligned after changes — a lag. Updating strategy needs engineering skill but the error is response latency. Prolonged misalignment risks inefficient spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration maintenance personnel remain unqualified after equipment or repair-requirement changes
rows.imp_rat: Personnel remaining unqualified after requirement changes is a training-lag gap (relevance) with moderate risk (severity), below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Change-triggered requalification is often ad hoc, leaving personnel unqualified for extended periods after changes. The need is the duration personnel remain unqualified after a change — a re-qualification lag. It concerns competence status but the error is the delay in restoring qualification. Requalification training carries cost that can slow its rollout.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in risk ratings assigned to equivalent equipment conditions
rows.imp_rat: Variation in risk ratings for equivalent conditions is a consistency issue (relevance) with moderate decision-risk (severity), below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Equivalent conditions get different risk ratings because rating is subjective and uncalibrated. Inconsistent risk ratings cause rework of prioritization decisions. Consistent rating demands calibrated expert judgement rarely standardized. Variable ratings misdirect resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that equipment risk priorities remain unchanged after production demands or conditions change
rows.imp_rat: Risk priorities unchanged after conditions change is a stale-prioritization gap (relevance) with moderate consequence (severity), below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Priority reviews are periodic, so ratings stay stale after conditions change. Risk priorities lag behind changing production demands for extended periods. Reprioritizing well requires risk-management judgement. Stale priorities misallocate maintenance spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time spent awaiting controlled-area access and equipment clearance
rows.imp_rat: Awaiting controlled-area access and clearance is a schedule delay to the repair (relevance) with recoverable consequence (severity), well below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Clearance processes are procedural and fairly consistent, though timing still varies. Awaiting controlled-area access and clearance adds significant fixed delay to every intervention. Navigating clearance procedures requires familiarity but little deep skill. Access delays extend costly downtime.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time required to obtain restart authorization after successful repair
rows.imp_rat: Time to obtain restart authorization after successful repair is a schedule delay (relevance) with recoverable cost (severity), well within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Restart approval flows exist but are subject to inconsistent availability of approvers. Obtaining restart authorization after a good repair still involves waiting on sign-offs. Approval requires qualified decision-makers, a limited pool. Authorization delay converts directly to lost output cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of equipment unavailability caused by incomplete restoration records
rows.imp_rat: Equipment unavailability from incomplete records extends downtime (relevance) with recoverable cost (severity), below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Incomplete records recurrently block availability despite the physical repair being done. Missing restoration records keep equipment idle awaiting paperwork completion. Completing records correctly requires procedural diligence. Documentation-caused idle time is lost production value.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that repeat mechanical failures are misclassified as isolated events in maintenance records
rows.imp_rat: Misclassifying repeat failures as isolated obscures recurring problems (relevance) with moderate latent risk (severity), below 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Maintenance records often log repeat failures as isolated events, obscuring true reliability trends. Misclassified repeats delay recognition of systemic problems. Correct classification needs analytical discipline in record-keeping. Misclassification hides recurring costs from view.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time required for restored equipment to reach stable operating conditions after return to service
rows.imp_rat: Time for restored equipment to reach stable operating conditions is a return-to-service delay (relevance) with recoverable impact (severity), below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Stabilization behavior is fairly predictable for known equipment, with occasional surprises. Reaching stable operating conditions after restart consumes ramp-up time before full output. Efficient stabilization depends on operator and technician skill. Extended stabilization delays return to revenue output.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of equipment release disruption caused by incomplete or conflicting restoration records
rows.imp_rat: Release disruption from conflicting records delays return-to-service (relevance) with recoverable impact (severity), under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Record inconsistencies are common and regularly hold up equipment release. Incomplete or conflicting records stall the release step and delay production restart. Reconciling records needs careful documentation discipline. Documentation-driven release delays cost production time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of intermittent mechanical faults that cannot be reproduced under observed equipment conditions
rows.imp_rat: Non-reproducible intermittent faults frustrate diagnosis (relevance) with moderate lingering-risk consequence (severity), under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Intermittent faults that cannot be reproduced remain a chronic, poorly served diagnostic problem. Intermittent faults extend diagnosis time, but the barrier is capability to reproduce them. Reproducing elusive intermittent faults is one of the hardest diagnostic skills and highly expert-dependent. Non-reproducible faults drive repeat visits and cost, yet skill is the binding limitation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict among operator accounts, maintenance records, and equipment-condition evidence
rows.imp_rat: Conflict among operator accounts and records complicates diagnosis (relevance) with moderate consequence (severity), comfortably below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Operator accounts, records, and physical evidence frequently conflict, and reconciliation is inconsistent. Reconciling conflicting accounts takes time, but the error is the conflict itself. Resolving discrepancies among operator, record, and physical evidence requires investigative judgement. Conflicts cause rework cost, but the core loss is unreliable, contradictory evidence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile-area access restrictions delaying examination of failed equipment
rows.imp_rat: Sterile-area access restrictions delaying examination slows diagnosis (relevance) with recoverable schedule impact (severity), below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Sterile-area access controls predictably delay examination, and expedited-access practices are only partially effective. This is directly about the duration of access restrictions delaying examination. Expediting access needs procedural know-how, but the loss experienced is waiting time. Access delays extend downtime cost, but the dominant concern is elapsed delay.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unplanned mechanical work-scope changes after integrity restoration begins
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned work-scope changes cause rework churn (relevance) with recoverable time impact (severity), comfortably below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Unplanned work-scope changes after start remain common, reflecting incomplete upfront assessment. Scope changes extend outages, but the error is the unplanned change itself, not the delay. Anticipating full scope up front demands strong diagnostic and planning expertise. Scope changes inflate cost and downtime, but the core failing is planning reliability.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of equipment release delay caused by unresolved mechanical verification findings
rows.imp_rat: Release delay from unresolved verification findings slows return-to-service (relevance) with recoverable cost (severity), below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Unresolved verification findings routinely stall release, and disposition processes are inconsistently fast. This need is explicitly about the duration of release delay from unresolved findings. Resolving findings quickly needs skilled disposition judgement, but the measured loss is delay. Delayed release ties up capacity at cost, yet duration is the dominant concern.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that cumulative effects of prior equipment changes are overlooked during impact assessment
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking cumulative prior-change effects is a subtle assessment gap (relevance) with moderate latent risk (severity), below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Cumulative-effect assessment is weak because change history is fragmented and often skipped. Reviewing cumulative change history takes extra time not always allotted. Assessing cumulative effects requires historical knowledge and judgement rarely consolidated. The review cost is modest versus the risk of ignored accumulation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that qualification extent lacks a documented rationale tied to maintenance impact and product risk
rows.imp_rat: Qualification extent lacking documented rationale is a justification gap (relevance) with moderate defensibility risk (severity), below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Qualification extent often lacks a documented risk rationale despite expectations. Documenting a risk-tied rationale for qualification extent adds authoring time. Justifying extent by maintenance impact and product risk requires risk-assessment expertise unevenly applied. Rationale documentation is inexpensive relative to audit consequences.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of acceptance criteria not objectively evaluable with qualified site capabilities
rows.imp_rat: Criteria not objectively evaluable with site capabilities blocks conclusive acceptance (relevance) with moderate consequence (severity), below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Non-evaluable criteria occasionally slip through, forcing rework or subjective judgement. Checking criteria against site capabilities adds a brief feasibility review. Ensuring criteria are objectively evaluable with site capabilities depends on knowing measurement limits, a specialized competence. If criteria exceed site capability, added metrology or outsourcing drives real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of qualification deviations unresolved when the recommendation is due
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved qualification deviations when recommendation is due delays closeout (relevance) with moderate consequence (severity), below 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Most qualification deviations are tracked to closure, but some remain open when recommendations are due. Clearing qualification deviations before the recommendation is due can compress timelines. Deviation resolution needs qualified reviewers. Investigation labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of replacement parts lacking required identity or installation traceability
rows.imp_rat: Replacement parts lacking identity/installation traceability drives rework/audit gaps (relevance) with moderate consequence (severity), under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Replacement-part identity and installation traceability is generally maintained but gaps recur for expedited parts. Recording part traceability adds documentation steps. Requires disciplined material traceability practice. Documentation-labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of restoration defects first discovered after equipment reassembly
rows.imp_rat: Defects first discovered after reassembly force costly rework (relevance) with recoverable schedule impact (severity), under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Defects first discovered after reassembly remain a recurring and expensive failure mode. Defects found after reassembly force costly rework cycles. Catching defects before reassembly requires skilled inspection. Post-reassembly discovery drives significant teardown and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of open actions or as-left conditions omitted from the restoration handoff
rows.imp_rat: Open actions omitted from handoff risks incomplete follow-through (relevance) with moderate consequence (severity), below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Open actions and as-left conditions are frequently omitted from handoffs, a common source of downstream surprises. Compiling open actions for handoff adds coordination time. Complete handoff depends on individual diligence. Coordination-labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to identify the failed mechanical condition after equipment becomes accessible
rows.imp_rat: Time to identify the failed condition affects repair speed, but ranking below batch 2 constrains proxies to the low end.
rows.sat_rat: Time to identify the fault varies widely with technician skill. Time to pinpoint the failed condition depends on diagnostic speed. Identifying the failed condition quickly hinges on scarce diagnostic expertise. Slow identification prolongs costly downtime.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration repaired equipment remains unavailable while required technical or quality decisions are pending
rows.imp_rat: Equipment unavailable while decisions pend delays return to service, but ranking keeps both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Pending decisions routinely hold repaired equipment out of service. Waiting on pending technical or quality decisions keeps equipment idle. Not skill-limited; a decision-latency issue. Decision delays extend downtime cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of reactive repair delay while equipment remains unavailable for safe maintenance access
rows.imp_rat: Reactive repair delay from unavailable access hampers restoration, but ranking constrains both proxies to the low end.
rows.sat_rat: Reactive repairs are often delayed awaiting safe maintenance access. Waiting for safe access delays the start of reactive repair. Not skill-limited; an access/handover constraint. Access delay extends costly downtime.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of lingering doubt that the original mechanical fault is resolved at equipment handback
rows.imp_rat: Lingering doubt the fault is resolved at handback touches core restoration confidence, but ranking caps proxies low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between fault notification and authorized physical access to sterile-area equipment
rows.imp_rat: Delay to authorized sterile-area access slows reactive repair, but the batch ceiling keeps both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Authorized access to sterile-area equipment is frequently slow. Delay from notification to authorized sterile-area access extends response. Not skill-limited; gowning and clearance procedures govern it. Access delay prolongs downtime cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required scheduled maintenance tasks left incomplete before equipment handback
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete required tasks before handback undermines restoration, but the batch ceiling keeps proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Some scheduled tasks are left incomplete before handback. Completing all scheduled tasks takes the planned time. Task completion depends on discipline and checklist use. Incomplete PM tasks cause later failures at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of delay before equipment is released for scheduled maintenance
rows.imp_rat: Delay before equipment release slows scheduled maintenance; consequence is modest and relevance capped by the batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Release for scheduled maintenance is moderately timely but variable. Delay before equipment release for scheduled work extends outage. Not skill-limited; a release-coordination issue. Release delay carries downtime cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration by which scheduled maintenance exceeds the approved equipment outage window
rows.imp_rat: Overrunning the approved outage window delays production, yet this delay error ranks below the prior batch, so relevance and severity stay minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduled maintenance regularly overruns its planned window. Overrunning the approved outage window is the core delay concern. Staying in window depends partly on planning and skill. Overruns cost lost production time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount by which maintenance work extends beyond agreed equipment downtime windows
rows.imp_rat: Work extending beyond agreed downtime windows is a delay, but ranking below batch 2 constrains proxies to the low band.
rows.sat_rat: Overruns are frequent because estimates and execution are imprecise. Work overrunning the agreed downtime window is a direct, common time overrun. Keeping to window depends on execution discipline and estimating skill. Overruns force production loss and overtime.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that equipment-release prerequisites remain incomplete when downtime begins
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete release prerequisites at downtime start cause delay; modest severity, relevance minimal under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Release prerequisites frequently remain open when downtime begins, a common status-quo failure. Unmet release prerequisites at downtime start directly waste the downtime window. Confirming prerequisites depends on dispatcher follow-up rather than automated gating. Idle crews during an unusable downtime window waste labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the work package omits required sterile-area contamination controls
rows.imp_rat: Work package omitting sterile contamination controls risks GMP breach, but the ceiling keeps both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Contamination-control inclusion is procedure-driven but package omissions still happen. Omitted contamination controls discovered late halt work and delay it. Knowing required controls demands sterile-area expertise. Missing controls risk costly contamination and re-cleaning.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a repair dispatch conflicts with sterile-production, cleaning, or environmental restrictions
rows.imp_rat: A dispatch conflicting with sterile-production restrictions poses contamination risk, but ranking below batch 2 holds proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Sterile-state constraints are partly captured in procedures but enforcement at dispatch is inconsistent. Dispatch conflicts with sterile restrictions cause stoppages that delay the whole sequence. Avoiding these conflicts depends on dispatcher knowledge of sterile/cleaning states. A conflicting dispatch can spoil product or force cleaning re-runs, a large cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that lower-criticality work is dispatched ahead of a production-critical mechanical fault
rows.imp_rat: Dispatching lower-criticality work ahead of a critical fault delays restoration, but ranking holds proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Criticality ranking exists informally but misordering still happens with some regularity. Dispatching low-criticality work first prolongs the outage of critical faults. Correct criticality ordering relies on dispatcher judgement absent a rigorous ranking system. Misordering extends costly production downtime.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that production equipment is not released by the scheduled maintenance start
rows.imp_rat: Equipment not released by scheduled start delays maintenance; severity modest, relevance minimal under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Release-by-start-time is unreliable because production handover timing is poorly controlled. Late equipment release directly consumes the scheduled maintenance start, a frequent delay source. Little special skill is needed; the issue is coordination timing with production. Crews standing by for unreleased equipment waste paid labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required replacement parts or consumables are unavailable at work start
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable parts at work start stalls repair, but the batch ordering caps relevance low and severity minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Kitting practices exist but parts availability at start remains frequently unmet. Missing parts at work start force stoppage and rescheduling, wasting the window. Parts staging needs planning skill but is not deeply expert work. Idle crews and expedited part orders waste cost.
