rows.sat_rat: Waiting for input delays but does not inherently distort the assessment. Outstanding stakeholder input delays impact assessment directly. Gathering timely input relies on coordination skill and stakeholder availability. Delay carries cost but the primary loss is assessment time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in qualification deliverables stated in the purchase recommendation
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in purchase-recommendation qualification deliverables is a documentation-clarity error, tangential to restoring integrity with modest stakes. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Ambiguity in stated qualification deliverables remains a recurring communication gap. Clarifying deliverables in the recommendation adds minor authoring time. Stating unambiguous deliverables requires clear technical-writing skill that varies. The authoring cost is small versus downstream misunderstanding.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that evidence is attributed to the wrong equipment, component, or restoration scope
rows.plain: Make sure the supporting information is tied to the correct equipment, part, and repair work. Otherwise, people could make a decision using evidence from something else.
rows.imp_rat: Evidence attributed to the wrong equipment or scope is an attribution defect, peripheral and low-consequence in this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Equipment-ID and tagging conventions make misattribution relatively uncommon. Verifying evidence attribution to the right equipment adds a check step. Correct attribution relies on labeling discipline rather than rare skill. The check cost is minor versus misattributed evidence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that restoration records have unverifiable authorship, chronology, or original values
rows.imp_rat: Restoration records with unverifiable authorship or chronology is a data-integrity gap, held low on both proxies by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Data-integrity/GDP controls and audit trails make unverifiable records fairly uncommon. Ensuring verifiable authorship and chronology adds documentation overhead. Maintaining attributable records relies on GDP discipline rather than scarce expertise. GDP practices cost little versus data-integrity findings.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts across maintenance, qualification, and equipment-history records
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflicts across records is a data-consistency defect, held low on both proxies by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Unresolved conflicts across maintenance, qualification and history records are common with siloed systems. Reconciling conflicting records across systems is time-consuming and often deferred. Resolving cross-record conflicts requires knowledgeable investigation seldom prioritized. Reconciliation labor is real but the felt loss is record conflict.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of restoration-related failure modes lacking defined post-release monitoring
rows.imp_rat: Failure modes lacking post-release monitoring is a follow-up gap, held low on both proxies here.
rows.sat_rat: Post-release monitoring for restoration failure modes is often undefined. Defining post-release monitoring adds planning time seldom prioritized. Designing monitoring for restoration failure modes requires reliability expertise that is scarce. Monitoring adds ongoing cost that discourages its definition.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material restoration-record gaps used to define the recommended approach
rows.imp_rat: Record gaps used to define the approach is a data-basis defect, peripheral and low-consequence in this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Restoration-record gaps used to define approaches remain a recurring issue. Filling material record gaps before scoping delays the approach definition. Recognizing and closing record gaps requires investigative diligence not always applied. Gap closure costs effort but the loss is a gap-based approach.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of approval delay caused by stakeholder disagreement over qualification scope
rows.imp_rat: Approval delay from scope disagreement is a coordination-delay error peripheral to physical repair; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Disagreement delays approval without inherently making the outcome wrong. Stakeholder disagreement over scope directly delays approval. Resolving disagreement relies on facilitation and expertise that vary. Delay carries indirect cost but the dominant loss is time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of qualification effort not justified by restoration risk
rows.imp_rat: Qualification effort not justified by risk is an efficiency issue, peripheral and low-consequence in this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Over-testing rarely harms outcome quality, so reliability is not the loss. Unjustified qualification effort extends timelines beyond the risk-based need. Right-sizing effort to risk requires risk-assessment expertise unevenly applied. Effort not justified by risk is direct wasted resource and labor spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in acceptance boundaries for the observed qualification failure
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in acceptance boundaries for a qualification failure is peripheral to core restoration and low-consequence here.
rows.sat_rat: Ambiguity in failure acceptance boundaries is a common recurring pain. Clarifying acceptance boundaries for a failure adds deviation-review time. Defining failure acceptance boundaries requires expert judgement often applied after the fact. The clarification cost is minor versus disposition ambiguity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of pending quality approvals after qualification evidence review is complete
rows.imp_rat: Pending quality approvals after review delays release, held low on both proxies by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Approvals eventually occur, so the issue is duration more than certainty. Pending quality approvals after review completion routinely stall for days, a chronic delay. Approval turnaround depends on reviewer availability rather than special skill. Delay ties up equipment, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration from restoration completion to required evidence review and approval
rows.imp_rat: Duration from completion to evidence review is a delay, peripheral and low-consequence within this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Review reliably happens eventually; the pain is how long it takes. The gap from restoration completion to evidence review is a well-known bottleneck with long queues. The delay reflects workflow queuing, not skill shortfall. Equipment downtime during the wait carries cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time required to retrieve the complete evidence package for a sampled mechanical restoration during inspection
rows.imp_rat: Time to retrieve evidence during inspection is an audit-efficiency issue, peripheral and low-consequence in this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Packages are usually assembled eventually, so the dominant pain is retrieval speed under inspection pressure. Retrieving a complete evidence package during inspection can take hours or days when records are dispersed across paper and systems. Retrieval depends on knowing where records live, a modest tacit skill. Search effort during inspection consumes staff time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required restoration records with an undefined or expired retention status
rows.imp_rat: Records with undefined retention status is a records-management gap, peripheral and low-consequence in this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Retention schedules are generally applied, but some records still have undefined or expired status. Setting retention status is a quick administrative step. Retention rules are defined and require little judgement. Records-management cost is budgeted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of waiting for required restoration evidence after the compliance assessment begins
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for restoration evidence after assessment begins is a delay, held low on both proxies here.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence usually arrives, so the core problem is the delay duration. Waiting for restoration evidence after the assessment begins commonly delays the compliance work. The wait is administrative, not skill-driven. Assessor idle time is a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when decision-makers challenge the restoration recommendation rationale
rows.plain: Stay calm when decision-makers question the reasons for recommending that something be restored. This helps the person give a clear explanation that can stand up to review.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when rationale is challenged is an emotional error, peripheral to restoring integrity with negligible consequence. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of feeling exposed when unable to answer a restoration traceability question
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed on a traceability question is an emotional error tangential to physical repair; ceiling applies.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of doubt triggered by conflicting evidence about the supplier's ability to follow documented quality controls
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about supplier quality-control ability is a supplier-assessment error tangential to restoring integrity; ceiling applies.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while awaiting confirmation that the recommended restoration scope satisfies applicable quality controls
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting scope-satisfies-controls confirmation is an emotional error peripheral to physical repair; ceiling caps it.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of being unable to explain the documented basis for the recommendation under inspector questioning
rows.plain: The person wants to explain the recorded reasons for a recommendation when an inspector questions it. A clear record helps show that the recommendation was based on sound evidence.
rows.imp_rat: Inability to explain recommendation basis under inspection is an audit-readiness gap, peripheral and low-consequence within this batch.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of visible uncertainty while linking the restoration recommendation to supporting quality records
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty linking recommendation to records is an emotional/clarity error tangential to physical restoration; ceiling applies.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of doubt arising from conflicting documentation about whether approved quality controls were followed
rows.imp_rat: Doubt from conflicting control documentation is a records-clarity error tangential to restoring integrity; ceiling caps it.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when an inspector challenges an unanticipated aspect of the recommendation
rows.plain: Stay calm when an inspector asks about an unexpected part of your recommendation. This helps you think clearly and explain how you reached it.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure at an inspector's challenge is a personal-emotional error, peripheral to the repair job and trivial in consequence. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of uncertainty about whether the recommended restoration conceals contamination or sterility hazards
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about concealed hazards is an assessment error tangential to executing the repair; ceiling caps it.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety spikes while awaiting evidence needed to resolve concealed restoration hazards
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting evidence for concealed hazards is an emotional error peripheral to physical repair; ceiling applies.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of reassurance lost when restoration evidence conflicts with contamination-control expectations
rows.imp_rat: Lost reassurance when evidence conflicts with expectations is a confidence error tangential to the repair; ceiling applies.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when unable to answer a hidden-hazard question before decision-makers
rows.plain: The person wants to identify and explain any hidden dangers before decision-makers approve the choice. This matters because leaders need confidence that important risks have not been missed.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed at a hidden-hazard question is purely emotional self-image, peripheral to the job with trivial consequence. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a post-restoration product-integrity failure is attributed to the influencer's recommendation
rows.plain: If the product is harmed after the equipment is restored, it should not be blamed on the person who recommended the purchase. The recommendation should not make that person seem responsible for a product-quality problem.
rows.imp_rat: Attribution of product-integrity failure to an influencer is reputational and downstream of the repair job; ceiling caps it.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of credibility lost with quality and validation stakeholders when restoration evaluation criteria are judged indefensible
rows.imp_rat: Credibility loss over evaluation criteria is reputational and tangential to restoring integrity; ceiling applies.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of public second-guessing of the restoration recommendation during cross-functional approval
rows.imp_rat: Public second-guessing during approval is a reputational error peripheral to physical restoration; ceiling applies.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of being bypassed by quality, engineering, or manufacturing leaders in future equipment-restoration decisions
rows.plain: The adviser wants to reduce the chance that quality, engineering, or production leaders leave them out of future decisions about restoring broken equipment. This helps preserve their influence over protecting the product.
rows.imp_rat: Being bypassed in future restoration decisions is reputational, not core integrity restoration; ceiling applies.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing credibility with inspectors when a restoration recommendation cannot withstand GMP scrutiny
rows.plain: Keep inspectors’ trust when a recommendation for restoring equipment or operations cannot be fully supported under strict manufacturing rules. This helps ensure the recommendation is revised or properly supported before approval.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility loss when a recommendation fails GMP scrutiny is a stakeholder-trust issue, peripheral and low-consequence within this batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of public blame from quality leadership for an inspection finding linked to an endorsed restoration decision
rows.plain: Avoid being publicly blamed by quality leaders for an inspection problem connected to a repair or restoration decision that was approved. The decision should be well supported and meet all required standards.
rows.imp_rat: Public blame for an inspection finding is reputational, downstream of repair; ceiling applies.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of being bypassed in future maintenance evaluations after restoration criteria are judged insufficient for sterile fill-finish GMP controls
rows.plain: The person wants to remain involved in future maintenance reviews by showing that repaired equipment has been restored well enough for safe, regulated medicine production. Otherwise, others may no longer trust their judgment.
rows.imp_rat: Being bypassed after insufficient criteria is reputational, not core integrity restoration; ceiling caps it.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of quality approvers second-guessing restoration recommendations because compliance controls are applied inconsistently
rows.imp_rat: Approvers second-guessing due to inconsistent controls is a governance/relationship error tangential to repair; ceiling caps it.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that quality leaders or inspectors regard the influencer as favoring expediency over documented GMP integrity
rows.imp_rat: Perception of favoring expediency over GMP is reputational, not core repair; ceiling caps it.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between restoration scope assumptions and actual equipment needs
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment planned for the restoration matches what will actually be needed. This helps prevent paying for the wrong equipment or discovering important needs too late.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between scope assumptions and actual needs is a scoping ambiguity, held low on both proxies here.
rows.sat_rat: Because the buyer inherits scope from others without a robust verification loop, mismatches between assumed and actual needs recur often. Scope clarification loops with maintenance engineers stretch the funding-preparation timeline when assumptions prove wrong. The purchase executor depends heavily on maintenance engineers' technical diagnosis and cannot independently validate restoration scope. Scope mismatches drive rework orders and wasted spare purchases that inflate the true cost of restoration.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of lifecycle ownership costs omitted from the restoration case
rows.imp_rat: Omitted lifecycle ownership costs is a business-case defect peripheral to physical repair; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Lifecycle costing is inconsistently done, so the restoration case commonly omits downstream ownership costs entirely. Building a lifecycle-cost view adds analysis time that reactive procurement rarely affords. Total-cost-of-ownership modeling requires analytical expertise that purchase executors seldom possess or apply. Omitting lifecycle ownership costs understates the true cost of restoration options and can lock in expensive long-term choices.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of error in production-loss exposure attributed to restoration downtime
rows.plain: Make the estimate of lost production caused by equipment-recovery stoppages as accurate as possible. This helps show the likely financial impact of those interruptions.
rows.imp_rat: Error in production-loss exposure estimation is a financial-analysis defect peripheral to restoration; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Production-loss estimates are often rough or assumption-driven, so error in the attributed exposure is common. Getting a defensible production-loss figure requires production-planning input that adds delay to case-building. Accurate downtime-cost attribution demands operational modeling skill the purchase executor does not hold. Errors in production-loss exposure distort the cost-benefit justification behind funding requests.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of batch-loss, contamination, and compliance exposure omitted from the restoration case
rows.imp_rat: Omitted batch-loss/contamination exposure from the case is a business-case defect peripheral to restoration; ceiling caps it.
rows.sat_rat: These exposures are rarely captured systematically, so the restoration case is frequently incomplete on risk grounds. Quantifying batch-loss and compliance exposure needs cross-functional input that slows the case when attempted. This requires quality and production risk expertise well outside the purchase executor's domain, making it heavily dependent on others. Leaving out contamination and compliance exposure understates the value at risk and skews funding decisions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of maintenance, failure, and cost records that are missing or contradictory
rows.imp_rat: Missing/contradictory maintenance records is a data-integrity defect tangential to executing the repair; ceiling caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Maintenance and cost records are chronically incomplete and inconsistent, corrupting the inputs to nearly every decision. Chasing down missing or contradictory records adds significant lookup time to every funding case. Reconciling conflicting maintenance, failure, and cost records requires investigative skill and institutional knowledge. Bad records lead to mis-scoped, mis-priced purchases that waste money on wrong or unnecessary items.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material cost-case assumptions disputed among maintenance, production, quality, procurement, and finance
rows.imp_rat: Disputed cost-case assumptions across functions is a financial-alignment error remote from restoration; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: No shared assumption framework exists, so cost-case disputes across functions arise frequently and inconsistently. Resolving disputed assumptions across five functions drags the case out through repeated meetings. Aligning maintenance, production, quality, procurement, and finance on cost assumptions demands facilitation and cross-domain judgement that is scarce. Disputed assumptions lead to padded or contested estimates that distort the committed spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of service, spare, and contract prices becoming outdated before funding approval
rows.plain: Avoid having prices for the service, replacement parts, and contract change before the money is approved. This helps create a reliable estimate of the full cost.
rows.imp_rat: Prices going outdated before approval is a procurement-timing error tangential to the repair; ceiling caps it.
rows.sat_rat: There is no systematic price-freshness alert, so stale pricing at approval recurs whenever cycles run long. The lag between quoting and funding approval is exactly where prices go stale, and approval cycles are slow enough to make this common. Little special skill is needed to note a price date, but tracking freshness across many lines is tedious and easily neglected. Outdated prices force re-quoting or acceptance of higher costs, eroding budget accuracy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of funding-case review caused by unanswered financial, production, quality, or maintenance questions
rows.imp_rat: Funding-case review delay from unanswered questions is a coordination-delay error peripheral to the repair; ceiling caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Review turnaround is unpredictable because it hinges on whichever question happens to stall the case. Unanswered financial, production, quality, or maintenance questions are the leading cause of funding-review delay, extending approval cycles. Anticipating and pre-answering reviewer questions requires broad cross-functional fluency the buyer rarely has alone. Review delays don't waste money directly but stall repairs, indirectly raising downtime cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of restoration expenditures classified inconsistently with finance policy
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent expenditure classification is a finance-policy defect peripheral to restoring integrity; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Finance policy exists and is applied with reasonable consistency, though ambiguous restoration cases still get classified inconsistently. Reclassification happens during finance review cycles, adding back-and-forth before spend is correctly booked. Applying capex-versus-opex and finance-policy classification correctly requires accounting expertise buyers rarely hold fully. Misclassification doesn't waste money directly but skews financial reporting and can trigger audit adjustments.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch among supplier proposals in restoration scope and commercial terms
rows.plain: Supplier offers should agree on what equipment or work will be restored and on the business terms. This lets the buyer compare the full cost fairly.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch among supplier proposals is a procurement-comparison error tangential to restoration; ceiling caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Without standardized RFQ templates, proposals arrive on different bases, so scope and term mismatches recur regularly. Reconciling mismatched supplier proposals adds comparison and clarification time before award. Normalizing scope and commercial terms across proposals takes sourcing skill that experienced buyers apply but juniors struggle with. Mismatched proposals hide true price differences and can lead to selecting a costlier or under-scoped option.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage by which committed mechanical-maintenance spend exceeds the approved budget envelope
rows.imp_rat: Committed spend exceeding budget envelope is a financial variance, peripheral to restoration and low-consequence in this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Standard PO-against-budget checks catch many cases but reactive scope and price changes still push committed spend past the envelope with regularity. Overrun detection typically lags to month-end financial close, well after the commitment is irreversible. Tracking committed-versus-approved requires finance fluency that many purchase executors apply inconsistently under pressure. Budget overruns are a chronic, visible pain that ERP commitment tracking only partially contains once reactive work spikes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of committed spend added by restoration scope changes after procurement approval
rows.imp_rat: Scope-change spend added after procurement is a cost-control error peripheral to the repair job; ceiling caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Change-order controls exist but are frequently bypassed when repairs are urgent, so added commitments slip through routinely. Post-approval scope changes force re-approval cycles that delay both the procurement and the repair. Judging which change orders are legitimate versus scope creep needs disciplined evaluation many buyers lack under time pressure. Change-driven add-on spend after approval is a common source of uncontrolled cost growth in reactive maintenance.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of supplier price escalation between budget approval and purchase commitment
rows.plain: Keep the supplier from raising the price between approval of the budget and placing the order. Price increases can make the approved budget insufficient.
rows.imp_rat: Supplier price escalation between approval and commitment is a procurement-cost error peripheral to repair; ceiling caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Some contracts include price-hold terms, but for one-off reactive buys escalation exposure remains largely uncontrolled. The escalation window is the gap between approval and commitment, which drags when procurement steps stack up. Negotiating price holds or locking rates takes commercial skill that stronger buyers use but not all do. Price escalation between approval and commitment directly increases spend beyond what was budgeted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of freight, taxes, and required support costs omitted from mechanical-maintenance budget estimates
rows.imp_rat: Omitted freight/tax/support from budget estimates is a budgeting defect peripheral to restoration; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Estimating templates capture some of these adders, but incidental support costs are still commonly left out. Freight and tax gaps are typically discovered at invoicing, requiring late budget top-ups. Estimating landed cost with all support charges takes experience that buyers build over time but apply inconsistently. Omitted freight, taxes, and support costs directly understate budgets and cause overruns on otherwise sound estimates.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of contract obligations omitted from the committed-spend total
rows.plain: Make sure all promises and payments in the contract are included when adding up the money already committed. This helps prevent the budget from looking smaller than it really is.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted contract obligations from committed-spend is a finance defect remote from repair execution; ceiling caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Contract-linked commitment capture is partial, so obligations outside standard POs are frequently missing from totals. Missing obligations often surface only at invoice time, too late to reflect in the committed-spend picture. Reading contracts for embedded ongoing obligations demands contract-analysis skill that purchase executors apply unevenly. Omitted obligations understate committed spend and create budget surprises when the bills eventually arrive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of maintenance commitments charged to incorrect approved budget envelopes
rows.imp_rat: Commitments charged to incorrect envelopes is an accounting defect, held low on both proxies by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: ERP defaults and templates reduce miscoding to an occasional, correctable error rather than a chronic failure. Miscoded charges are usually found during periodic reconciliation rather than at commitment time, adding correction delay. Correct cost-center and WBS selection demands familiarity with a sprawling coding structure that trips up less-experienced buyers. Wrong-envelope charges distort budget visibility but the total money is not lost, only misattributed and later corrected.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of committed spend duplicated across orders or contracts for the same maintenance requirement
rows.plain: Do not pay twice for the same maintenance work through different orders or contracts. This prevents unnecessary spending.
rows.imp_rat: Duplicated committed spend across orders is procurement waste, only indirectly tied to restoration; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: ERP and contract catalogs catch some overlaps but siloed reactive purchasing still lets duplicate orders through. Duplicate detection usually happens late, after parallel orders have already been committed. Spotting duplication across separate orders and contracts requires cross-referencing skill and vigilance that is hard to sustain. Duplicated commitments for the same requirement are direct wasted spend, a recognized leakage in fragmented procurement.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of approved budget held by commitments for canceled or deferred maintenance work
rows.imp_rat: Budget held by canceled-work commitments is a finance-waste error remote from restoration execution; ceiling caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Commitment closure is inconsistent, so budget frequently stays tied up in dead or deferred work orders. Releasing commitments for canceled work relies on periodic clean-ups rather than prompt closure. Identifying stale commitments needs disciplined portfolio review that buyers perform irregularly. Approved budget locked in canceled or deferred commitments is idle money that blocks other needed spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of supplier quotes expiring before purchase approval for mechanical-maintenance requirements
rows.imp_rat: Quotes expiring before approval is a procurement-timing error peripheral to the repair; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Quote-expiry tracking is manual and inconsistent, so expirations before approval happen frequently. Quote validity windows routinely expire during multi-step approval, the core symptom being approval taking longer than the quote lasts. Monitoring expiry dates is low-skill but requires diligent follow-up that competes with other tasks. Expired quotes trigger re-quoting and often higher prices, adding avoidable cost and effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of required purchase approvals or control records being missing at commitment
rows.plain: Avoid reaching the point of committing to a purchase without all required approvals and control records. Complete records show that the purchase was properly reviewed and authorized.
rows.imp_rat: Missing purchase approvals at commitment is a control-record gap, held low on both proxies by the ranking.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt caused by conflicting procurement, finance, and quality guidance during the purchase
rows.plain: The person buying the service needs one clear set of instructions from the buying, finance, and quality teams. Conflicting guidance could create mistakes or problems during a later review.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt from conflicting procurement/finance/quality guidance is a coordination-ambiguity error, marginal to physical restoration and low in stakes. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while control confirmation remains pending before purchase commitment
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety while control confirmation is pending is an emotional-timing error, peripheral to the repair job with trivial stakes. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when the purchase rationale and supporting records cannot be explained on demand
rows.plain: The person wants to quickly explain why the purchase was made and show the records that support it. This matters because missing or confusing records can make a valid decision look improper during an audit.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed when purchase rationale can't be explained is an emotional-confidence error, remote from the physical job and low in consequence. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of visible uncertainty about purchase authorization while sterile production is stalled
rows.plain: The team needs a quick answer about whether the purchase is approved while clean production is stopped. A fast decision can help limit the time the production line is idle.
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty about purchase authorization while production stalled is a delay error, modestly relevant but capped at 1.0 by the batch ceiling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety spikes while critical pricing, availability, or contract information remains outstanding
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety spikes while pricing/availability info is outstanding is an emotional error, tangential to the physical job with trivial consequence. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of unease caused by conflicting maintenance, quality, production, procurement, and finance input
rows.plain: Quickly resolve conflicting advice from maintenance, quality, production, purchasing, and finance teams. This helps the group make a decision before equipment downtime lasts longer.
rows.imp_rat: Unease from conflicting cross-function input is an emotional/coordination error, remote from restoring integrity and low in consequence. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when stakeholders demand a decision before the procurement path is clear
rows.plain: The person wants the purchasing steps to be clear before being forced to make a decision. This matters because rushing without knowing the process could create delays, mistakes, or personal blame.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed when a decision is demanded pre-procurement-clarity is an emotional error, remote from the repair job with trivial stakes. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt that the commercial commitment is defensible at sign-off
rows.plain: Make sure the price and business agreement can be properly justified when the purchase receives final approval. This allows the decision-maker to stand behind the cost.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt over commercial-commitment defensibility is a commercial-confidence error, marginal to physical restoration and low in stakes. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of visible uncertainty when approvers challenge the pricing rationale
rows.plain: The buyer needs to quickly explain why the quoted price is reasonable when approvers challenge it. This helps support a confident purchasing decision.
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty when challenged on pricing is a communication/confidence error, peripheral to the physical job with trivial consequence. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety spikes while conflicting commercial-term interpretations remain unresolved
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety over unresolved commercial-term interpretations is an emotional error, remote from the repair job with trivial stakes. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when material commercial questions cannot be answered at sign-off
rows.plain: They want to avoid feeling exposed when important money-related questions remain unanswered at final approval. They need enough information to stand behind the cost and benefit claims.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed at unanswered commercial questions is a personal-confidence error, remote from the repair job and trivial in consequence. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of distress under pressure to approve last-minute pricing or term changes
rows.imp_rat: Distress under pressure to approve last-minute changes is an emotional error, peripheral to the physical repair with trivial consequence. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that maintenance and production leaders attribute restoration delay to slow purchase approval
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that maintenance and production leaders blame slow purchasing approval for a delayed repair or restart. Important purchases should move quickly enough to help restore operations.
rows.imp_rat: Attribution of delay to slow purchase approval is a blame/reputation error, remote from the physical job and low in consequence. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of credibility loss after promised funding or procurement decision deadlines are missed for urgent restoration needs
rows.imp_rat: Credibility loss after missed funding deadlines is a reputation error, remote from restoring integrity and low in consequence. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which maintenance or production leaders bypass the designated buyer for urgent restoration purchases
rows.imp_rat: Bypassing the designated buyer for urgent purchases is a procurement-governance error, remote from the physical job and low in consequence. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost when the financial basis for an urgent acquisition is challenged after approval
rows.plain: They want to avoid seeming financially careless when people question the reasons for an urgent purchase after it has been approved. This matters because the emergency decision still needs to be understandable and defensible.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when financial basis is challenged is a reputation error, remote from the physical job and low in consequence. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which maintenance and production leaders second-guess urgency judgments across restoration needs
rows.imp_rat: Second-guessing urgency judgments is a decision-consistency error, peripheral to the physical repair and low in consequence. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that finance and service-line leaders assign blame for a restoration acquisition exceeding its approved budget
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that finance and service leaders blame the person who made the purchase if buying restoration equipment costs more than approved. This matters because the buyer needs to show that the spending was properly planned and controlled.
rows.imp_rat: Blame for exceeding approved budget is a reputation/politics error, remote from the physical repair and low in consequence. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost when a restoration deal is perceived as sacrificing lifecycle value for near-term savings
rows.plain: Protect trust by showing that restoring the equipment does not trade long-term usefulness and value for a small short-term saving. The repair or replacement should make sense over the equipment's full useful life.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of projected financial benefit duplicated across availability, yield, and avoided-loss calculations

rows.sat_rat: Benefit double-counting is a common and hard-to-detect modeling flaw. Benefit double-counting is uncovered late during business-case scrutiny. Separating overlapping availability, yield, and avoided-loss benefits requires sophisticated financial-modeling skill. Duplicated benefit inflates the case and can justify overspending.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material baseline assumptions disputed by finance, production, and mechanical maintenance stakeholders
rows.imp_rat: Disputed baseline assumptions across functions is an estimating-ambiguity error, tangential to restoring integrity with modest stakes. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Stakeholder baseline disputes are frequent given misaligned functional priorities. Baseline disputes among stakeholders prolong the approval timeline. Aligning finance, production, and maintenance baselines requires cross-functional negotiation skill. Disputed baselines undermine the credibility and sizing of the funding case.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of production schedule changes that invalidate maintenance timing assumptions in the funding case
rows.imp_rat: Schedule changes invalidating funding timing assumptions is a planning-instability error, modestly relevant but capped at 1.0 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Production schedule changes regularly break maintenance timing assumptions. Schedule changes invalidate timing assumptions with little warning. Keeping funding-case timing aligned with production changes requires ongoing coordination. Invalidated timing assumptions cause maintenance to be funded at the wrong moment.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to resolve review questions before mechanical maintenance budget authorization
rows.imp_rat: Time to resolve review questions before authorization is a process-latency error, modestly relevant but capped at 1.0 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Review-question cycles are variable and often protracted. Resolving review questions before authorization is a frequent source of approval delay. Answering review questions quickly depends on requester and reviewer expertise availability. Delayed resolution prolongs downtime and can raise repair cost.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of maintenance expenditures lacking supporting financial records
rows.imp_rat: Expenditures lacking supporting financial records is a records-completeness error, tangential to restoring integrity with compliance-cost stakes. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Documentation is generally kept but gaps in supporting records still occur. Missing financial records surface only during audit or reconciliation. Retaining complete supporting records is routine clerical discipline, unevenly maintained. Unsupported expenditures risk disallowance and audit findings.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of authorized maintenance funds spent outside approved scope
rows.plain: Use approved maintenance money only for the work it was authorized to cover. This helps show that funds are being used responsibly.
rows.imp_rat: Funds spent outside approved scope is a budget-governance error, tangential to restoring integrity with financial-only stakes. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Scope discipline is imperfect, so some authorized funds drift to unapproved uses. Out-of-scope spend is usually caught only at reconciliation, well after it occurs. Enforcing scope adherence requires diligent oversight of what funds are actually used for. Funds spent outside approved scope waste budget and undermine the funding basis.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of maintenance expenditures not traceable to specific work orders and production equipment
rows.imp_rat: Expenditures not traceable to work orders/equipment is a financial-traceability error, tangential to restoring integrity with compliance-cost stakes. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Work-order traceability is partial, leaving a meaningful share of spend unlinked. Traceability to work orders is checked at reporting time, not in real time. Maintaining work-order-to-cost linkage requires disciplined CMMS/ERP practice. Untraceable expenditures obscure where money went and enable leakage.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of maintenance charges inconsistent with approved prices, quantities, or completed work
rows.plain: Prevent charges for upkeep that do not match the agreed prices, amounts, or work actually completed. This helps ensure the organization pays only what it should.
rows.imp_rat: Charges inconsistent with approved prices is a finance/invoice defect remote from the physical repair; ceiling caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Standard three-way-match practice reliably flags most mismatches, though some slip through on services. Three-way match at invoice time is reasonably prompt within standard AP cycles. PO-to-invoice matching is a well-defined clerical control requiring modest skill. Matching controls catch most charges inconsistent with approved prices and quantities, limiting waste.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of duplicate or erroneous payments for maintenance purchases
rows.imp_rat: Duplicate/erroneous payments is a finance-waste error remote from the repair job; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Mature AP duplicate-payment controls are widely deployed and catch the large majority of cases. Duplicate-payment detection runs on standard AP cycles, catching most before or shortly after payment. Duplicate detection is largely automated in modern AP systems, needing little specialized skill. Automated duplicate checks recover or prevent most erroneous payments, limiting cost leakage.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of maintenance expenditures assigned to the wrong asset, cost center, or budget category
rows.imp_rat: Expenditures assigned to the wrong asset/cost center is a cost-accounting error, tangential to restoring integrity with monetary-only stakes. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Coding errors to wrong asset or cost center are a common recurring data-quality issue. Miscoding is usually corrected at reconciliation, after the fact. Correct cost-center and asset coding requires attentive data entry that varies in quality. Misassigned expenditures distort cost visibility and misinform funding decisions.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of unexplained variance between the authorized maintenance budget and recorded spending
rows.plain: Make unexplained differences between the approved repair budget and actual spending as small as possible. This shows where the money went and supports responsible oversight.
rows.imp_rat: Unexplained variance between budget and spending is a financial-accountability error, tangential to restoring integrity with monetary stakes. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Variance analysis is standard but often leaves material amounts unexplained. Variance explanation happens at period close, lagging the actual spend. Explaining budget-to-actual variance requires analytical skill that some teams lack. Unexplained variance signals uncontrolled spend that erodes budget confidence.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of maintenance commitments and liabilities omitted from reported spending
rows.plain: Make sure all promised future maintenance costs and obligations appear in spending reports. This gives a truthful picture of what the organization still owes.
rows.imp_rat: Commitments omitted from reported spending is a reporting defect, held low on both proxies by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Reported spending routinely omits open POs and accruals, making the reported position unreliable. Commitments and liabilities are often recorded late, so reported spending lags real obligations by weeks. Capturing all open commitments requires disciplined encumbrance accounting that many maintenance groups perform inconsistently. Omitted liabilities understate true spend and lead to unfunded surprises that cost more to absorb later.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Reduce the time to reconcile and report maintenance spending after work completion
rows.imp_rat: Time to reconcile spending after completion is a reporting-latency error, tangential to the physical job with monetary-only consequence. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Reconciliation eventually completes but is consistently slow relative to need. Reconciling and reporting spend after completion follows month-end cycles and can take weeks. Post-completion reconciliation is routine accounting work of moderate skill. Slow reconciliation delays freeing or reallocating residual funds.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of maintenance expenditures lacking evidence that funded work restored mechanical integrity
rows.imp_rat: Expenditures lacking evidence work restored integrity is an assurance/traceability error, modestly relevant but capped at 1.0 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Many expenditures lack evidence tying spend to restored integrity, a common gap. Evidence of restored integrity, when required, is gathered after work completion. Producing integrity-restoration evidence requires engineering verification effort often skipped. Spending without integrity evidence risks paying for work that did not deliver.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of future mechanical maintenance obligations for sterile fill-finish equipment omitted from the funding forecast
rows.imp_rat: Omitted future obligations from forecast is a budgeting defect remote from restoration execution; ceiling caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Forecasts commonly omit future fill-finish obligations, leaving the plan incomplete and surprise-prone. Future obligations for fill-finish equipment are often recognized late in planning cycles. Projecting future sterile-equipment maintenance obligations needs specialized asset-lifecycle expertise. Omitting future obligations understates the forecast and leaves later integrity work unfunded.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Reduce the amount by which forecast funding understates included mechanical maintenance obligations
rows.imp_rat: Forecast understating obligations is a budgeting defect remote from physical restoration; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Forecasts frequently understate included obligations, an established recurring weakness. Understated obligations are discovered only when invoices exceed forecast, well after approval. Aligning forecast to full included obligations requires disciplined estimating judgement that varies. Forecast understatement of known obligations produces predictable funding shortfalls.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that unplanned mechanical failures create obligations beyond forecast funding
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned failures creating budget overruns is a financial-instability error remote from restoration; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Unplanned failures routinely exceed forecast funding, a chronic and largely unmitigated condition. Unplanned failures by definition arrive with no lead time, outpacing forecast provisioning. Estimating the rate of unforeseen obligations requires reliability-engineering judgement seldom fed into funding. Failures beyond forecast create unbudgeted obligations that overrun the approved envelope.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of the gap between a maintenance obligation becoming due and its forecast funding becoming available
rows.imp_rat: Gap between obligation due and forecast funding available is a funding-latency error, modestly relevant but capped at 1.0 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: The due-versus-available gap recurs and is poorly synchronized. The gap between an obligation becoming due and forecast funding availability is a chronic timing mismatch. Bridging the timing gap requires cash-flow forecasting skill inconsistently applied. Timing gaps force costly interim funding or deferral.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of in-scope production equipment with condition evidence too old to support funding assumptions
rows.imp_rat: Equipment with condition evidence too old for funding assumptions is a data-currency error, tangential to the physical repair with modest stakes. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: A meaningful share of equipment lacks current condition evidence to support assumptions. Condition evidence ages between inspections, and refreshes lag funding cycles. Judging whether condition data is current enough requires reliability expertise. Stale condition evidence leads to mis-sized or misdirected spending.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of labor, material, and contracted-service escalation excluded from the maintenance funding forecast
rows.imp_rat: Excluded escalation from funding forecast is a budgeting defect remote from repair execution; ceiling caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Escalation is inconsistently modeled, so forecasts routinely lag actual cost growth. Escalation is typically ignored until prices actually rise, too late for the forecast. Forecasting labor, material, and service escalation requires market analysis skill rarely applied to maintenance. Excluded escalation understates forecast cost and forces mid-cycle funding gaps as prices climb.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of disagreement among scope and cost inputs for the same mechanical maintenance obligation
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among scope/cost inputs for one obligation is an estimating-ambiguity error, tangential to the physical repair with modest stakes. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Scope and cost inputs frequently conflict for the same obligation. Reconciling conflicting scope and cost inputs adds delay to decisions. Resolving input disagreements requires facilitation and technical judgement. Disagreement on inputs risks funding wrong amounts.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between equipment-utilization assumptions and planned sterile fill-finish demand
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between utilization assumptions and demand is a planning-accuracy error, tangential to the physical repair with modest stakes. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Utilization and demand assumptions frequently diverge from actuals. Utilization-versus-demand mismatches emerge as plans shift over time. Aligning utilization assumptions with demand needs planning coordination skill. Mismatched assumptions misprioritize which equipment justifies maintenance spend.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of forecast funding duplicated across line items for the same mechanical maintenance obligation
rows.imp_rat: Duplicated forecast funding across line items is a budgeting-accuracy error, tangential to restoring integrity with monetary-only stakes. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Forecast duplication occurs and is inconsistently reconciled. Duplicated forecast line items are found during forecast review cycles if at all. Detecting duplicated forecast lines requires disciplined forecast hygiene skill. Duplicated forecast lines overstate obligations and distort budget sizing.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that regulatory or quality changes create mechanical maintenance obligations absent from the approved forecast
rows.imp_rat: Regulatory changes creating unforecast obligations is a financial-planning error remote from restoration; ceiling caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory-driven obligations frequently appear outside the approved forecast, a recurring gap. Regulatory changes create obligations on timelines disconnected from the approved budget cycle. Translating regulatory or quality changes into funding obligations requires niche compliance-cost expertise. New compliance obligations absent from the forecast force unbudgeted spending.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of unbudgeted emergency expenditure triggered by unexpected sterile fill-finish equipment failures
rows.plain: Avoid unexpected emergency spending when equipment used to fill sterile medicines fails. Planning and reliable equipment can help prevent costly urgent repairs or replacements.
rows.imp_rat: Unbudgeted emergency expenditure is a financial-instability error remote from executing the repair; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Such emergency spend recurs and is poorly buffered, making it a persistent budget shock. Emergency fill-finish failures demand immediate spend with no time to secure planned funding. Anticipating which fill-finish failures will trigger emergencies requires deep equipment knowledge rarely captured in budgets. Unexpected fill-finish failures trigger costly unbudgeted emergency expenditure, often at premium rates.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of authorizing a maintenance purchase without accounting for existing budget commitments
rows.plain: The buyer wants to reduce the chance of approving a repair purchase without considering money already promised for other work. This helps prevent unexpected overspending.
rows.imp_rat: Authorizing purchase without accounting for commitments is a budget-control ambiguity, peripheral and low-consequence in this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Encumbrance tracking in ERP mitigates this but gaps and manual overrides make it a recurring error. Committed-but-unspent amounts update on a lag, so a purchase can be authorized before the system reflects prior commitments. Avoiding this requires the approver to check open encumbrances manually, a step easily skipped under pressure. Authorizing without netting commitments causes double-spending against the same envelope and unexpected overruns.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of freight, taxes, and ancillary service expenditure omitted from maintenance funding estimates
rows.plain: Include delivery charges, taxes, and extra service costs in the money set aside for maintenance. This helps keep maintenance spending within the budget.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted freight/tax in estimates is a budgeting defect peripheral to physical restoration; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: These small-but-material items are habitually left out, a consistent estimation gap. Freight, taxes, and ancillary charges surface late in invoicing rather than at estimate time. Anticipating ancillary charges requires procurement detail knowledge often absent from the estimate. Omitted freight, taxes, and ancillary spend directly understate true cost and cause budget shortfalls.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of maintenance expenditure added after mechanical restoration scope expands
rows.plain: Keep maintenance spending from growing beyond the budget when repair work becomes more extensive than expected. Any added work should be identified and controlled.
rows.imp_rat: Added expenditure after scope expansion is a cost-control error, tangential to physical restoration with financial-only stakes. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Scope creep on mechanical restoration is common and poorly contained. Scope-expansion costs appear after work starts, too late to re-plan funding. Predicting scope growth requires engineering foresight often lacking at estimate time. Added expenditure from scope expansion inflates spend beyond the authorized amount.
